summary: Large language models (LLMs) reliably predict neural activity during language comprehension and transformer depth has been interpreted as mirroring hierarchical cortical organization. However, it remains unclear whether such alignment extends to subcortical regions, overlaps spatially across languages, and what the computational roots of such alignment are. Here, we used a multilingual, whole-brain encoding framework to examine brain-LLM alignment across three typologically distinct languages: Mandarin, English, and French during naturalistic story listening. Our results show that across languages, transformer-based models predicted activity in a distributed landscape spanning widely distributed cortical functional networks like limbic, ventral attention, default mode network, and subcortical structures. Spatial alignment patterns showed substantial cross-linguistic overlap and remained largely stable across model layers, with limited layer progression consistent with functional cortical hierarchies. Contrary to previous evidence, contextual embeddings did not outperform static embeddings. To test candidate computational explanations, we examined whether layer-wise brain scores reflect surprisal and intrinsic dimensionality, and thereby predictive processing and information compression. Neither of these two computational metrics mirrored neural alignment profiles. Our findings suggest that brain-LLM alignment is spatially robust and cross-linguistically stable but not explainable from predictive uncertainty or representational geometry. Rather than directly reflecting shared hierarchical computation, neural predictivity may primarily arise from distributed lexical-semantic correspondences that generalize across languages.

AI.tldr: This study explores the alignment between large language models (LLMs) and brain neural activity during language comprehension across three languages, finding cross-linguistic and spatial stability, but limited evidence of hierarchical computational explanations.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to explore whether the alignment between LLMs and neural activity extends to subcortical regions, crosses linguistic boundaries, and reflects computational principles such as predictive uncertainty or representational geometry.
AI.method: A whole-brain, multilingual encoding framework was applied to examine brain-LLM alignment during naturalistic story listening in Mandarin, English, and French using transformer-based models.
AI.result: LLMs predicted neural activity across cortical and subcortical regions with spatially robust patterns that were stable across languages. Contextual embeddings did not outperform static embeddings, and alignment profiles were not well-explained by surprisal or intrinsic dimensionality.
AI.conclusion: Brain-LLM alignment is consistent across languages and brain regions but is not due to computational principles like predictive processing or intrinsic dimensionality. The alignment appears to reflect distributed lexical-semantic correspondences rather than hierarchical computation.

title: Comparative Analysis of Military Detection Using Drone Imagery Across Multiple Visual Spectrums
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures. Accepted at the 16th International Conference on Computing, Communication and Networking Technologies (ICCCNT), July 6-11, 2025, IIT Indore. Proceedings pending publication
summary: In modern warfare, drones are becoming an essential part of intelligence gathering and carrying out precise attacks in different kinds of hostile environments. Their ability to operate in real-time and hostile environments from a safe distance makes them invaluable for surveillance and military operations. The KIIT-MiTA dataset is comprised of images of different military scenarios taken from drones, and these provide a foundation for detecting military objects, but it does not take into account the various types of real-world scenarios. With that in mind, to evaluate how the models are performing under varying conditions, four different types of datasets are created: Gray Scale, Thermal Vision, Night Vision, and Obscura Vision. These simulate the real-world environments such as low visibility, heat-based imagery, and nighttime conditions. The YOLOv11-small model is trained and used to detect objects across diverse settings. This research boosts the performance and reliability of drone-based operations by contributing to the development of advanced detection systems in both defensive and offensive missions.

AI.tldr: The paper focuses on improving drone-based object detection in military scenarios through the creation of diverse datasets and utilizing the YOLOv11-small model.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets like KIIT-MiTA lack coverage of diverse real-world scenarios crucial for military applications, thus making it necessary to establish more versatile datasets and test models under varied conditions.
AI.method: The researchers created four different datasets simulating real-world military scenarios: Gray Scale, Thermal Vision, Night Vision, and Obscura Vision. They trained and tested the YOLOv11-small object detection model on these datasets.
AI.result: The YOLOv11-small model demonstrated the capability to detect military objects effectively, even under challenging conditions such as low visibility and nighttime environments.
AI.conclusion: This study enhances the effectiveness and reliability of drone-based intelligence and operational systems by improving object detection capabilities in diverse and challenging settings.

title: SAVER: Selective As-Needed Vision Evidence for Multimodal Information Extraction
summary:
Multimodal IE in social media is difficult because a post may attach multiple images that are weakly related, redundant, or even misleading with respect to the text. In this setting, always-on multimodal fusion wastes computation and can amplify spurious visual cues. The core challenge is to decide, for each candidate span or marked entity pair, whether vision should be consulted at all and, if so, which small subset of images provides trustworthy evidence.
  We propose SAVER, a selective vision-as-needed framework for multimodal named entity recognition and multimodal relation extraction. SAVER uses a Conformal Groundability Gate (CGG) to estimate span-level visual groundability in MNER, derive pair-level activation in MRE from the two marked entities, and calibrate the activation threshold on a held-out split via a conformal-style procedure with Clopper--Pearson upper bounds. When activated, a submodular relevance--diversity selector chooses a compact evidence subset across images, which is then aggregated by a Set Transformer. An energy-inspired joint scoring head combines text, optional visual evidence, text--image consistency, and sparse routing for entity typing or relation classification.
  Experiments show that SAVER consistently improves F1 over strong text-only and always-on multimodal baselines, while reducing AURC, increasing activation coverage at a fixed risk level, and lowering FLOPs and P90 latency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SAVER, a system for multimodal information extraction in social media posts, selectively using visual content only when necessary to improve efficiency and effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal information extraction methods struggle with social media posts that attach multiple images, as many of these images are weakly related, redundant, or misleading to the accompanying text. This leads to wasted computation and the potential amplification of spurious visual cues.
AI.method: The proposed SAVER framework includes a Conformal Groundability Gate (CGG) for estimating the relevance of visual information at the span or entity pair level. When visual information is deemed necessary, a submodular selection process picks the most relevant and diverse images to use. This system uses a Set Transformer to integrate the selected images and a joint scoring head to combine text, visual evidence, and other factors for named entity recognition or relation classification.
AI.result: SAVER outperforms both text-only and always-on multimodal baselines in F1 score, reduces computational costs (lower AURC, FLOPs, and latency), and increases activation coverage while maintaining a fixed risk level.
AI.conclusion: SAVER is an efficient and effective approach to selectively incorporating visual information for multimodal named entity recognition and relation extraction in social media, addressing the challenges of irrelevant or misleading visual content.

title: APM: Evaluating Style Personalization in LLMs with Arbitrary Preference Mappings
summary: Typical LLM responses tend to follow a default style, even though users often have distinct preferences regarding tone, verbosity, and formality that they do not explicitly state in their prompts. Evaluating whether personalization methods can adapt to these implicit preferences is challenging, since users typically provide prompts rather than reference responses, style preferences are not factually verifiable, and reference-free LLM judges may conflate personalization with general response quality. To address these challenges, we introduce the Arbitrary Preference Mapping (APM) benchmark, which decouples user attributes (e.g. enthusiastic) from response principles (e.g. persuasive) via a hidden, randomized mapping $\mathbf{C}$ that maps user attributes to preferences about response traits. Because $\mathbf{C}$ carries no semantic content and is resampled across runs, models cannot exploit stereotypical associations and must infer preferences from conversation history. Using this unbiased evaluation methodology, we adapt retrieval-augmented, prompt-optimization, and routing personalization methods and evaluate them on Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen-3.5-27B. Our results show that routing is the most reliable approach, while RAG only improves with the stronger base LLM, and soft prompt optimization fails to improve significantly over a non-personalized baseline. Our extensive evaluation reveals that in this realistic setting, personalization remains challenging, but our adapted methods show promise.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Arbitrary Preference Mapping (APM) to assess personalized response generation in LLMs under unbiased settings.
AI.motivation: Addressing the challenge of adapting LLM responses to implicit user preferences like tone, verbosity, and formality.
AI.method: Introduction of the Arbitrary Preference Mapping benchmark and adaptation of personalization methods like retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), prompt optimization, and routing.
AI.result: Routing emerged as the most reliable personalization method, whereas RAG benefited with stronger base LLMs, and soft prompt optimization underperformed.
AI.conclusion: Personalization using implicit attributes is difficult, but adapted methods under APM show potential for improvement in realistic evaluation scenarios.

title: Efficient Table QA via TableGrid Navigation and Progressive Inference Prompting
comment: Accepted for Presentation in ICDAR 2026, Vienna, Austria
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promising results on NLP tasks, however, their performance on tabular data still needs research attention, because Table Question-Answering (TQA) requires precise cell retrieval and multi-step structured reasoning. Existing work improves TQA either by fine-tuning or training LLMs on task-specific tabular data, but often lacks verifiable control over how the model navigates tables and derives answers. In this work, we propose a training-free TQA approach with two structured prompting frameworks: TableGrid Navigation (TGN), which iteratively navigates rows and columns via a three-module loop to locate evidence and refine answers, and Progressive Inference Prompting (PIP), which enforces columns identification for explicit progressive row selection constraint according to the query. We evaluate 17 LLMs against 6 baselines on TableBench and FeTaQa dataset. On TableBench, TGN improves over the strongest baseline by 3.8 points, and on FeTaQa, PIP achieves SOTA performance over ReAct and Chain-of-Thought. Beyond inference-time gains, PIP and TGN can also serve as supervision templates to fine-tune small models, narrowing the performance gap to much larger architectures in resource-constrained settings, offering versatile and cost-efficient solution for TQA.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a training-free approach for Table Question-Answering (TQA) using structured prompting instead of fine-tuning large models, achieving significant improvements and offering cost-efficient solutions.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with Table Question-Answering (TQA), which demands precise cell retrieval and multi-step structured reasoning.
AI.method: The authors introduce two structured prompting frameworks: TableGrid Navigation (TGN) for iterative navigation and evidence retrieval, and Progressive Inference Prompting (PIP) for enacting progressive row constraints.
AI.result: TGN improves over baselines by 3.8 points on TableBench, while PIP achieves state-of-the-art performance on FeTaQa datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods not only enhance LLM performance during inference but also serve as a cost-efficient way to fine-tune smaller models for TQA tasks.

title: Hyper-V2X: Hypernetworks for Estimating Epistemic and Aleatoric Uncertainty in Cooperative Bird's-Eye-View Semantic Segmentation
comment: Accepted for IEEE Intelligent Vehicle Symposium (IV) 2026
summary: Cooperative perception enabled by Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication enhances autonomous driving safety by creating a unified environmental representation through shared sensory data. While recent works have advanced multi-agent fusion for improved perception, uncertainty quantification in such cooperative frameworks remains largely unexplored. This paper introduces Hyper-V2X, a hypernetwork-based framework for estimating both epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties in V2X-based perception. Specifically, we propose a partial weight generation scheme and V2X context embedding module that conditions a Bayesian hypernetwork on fused multi-agent features to generate weight distributions for stochastic Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) segmentation. Unlike existing deterministic BEV models, Hyper-V2X enables efficient uncertainty estimation with little computation overhead. Our approach is architecture-agnostic, and can be seamlessly integrating with modern cooperative backbones such as CoBEVT. Experiments on the OPV2V benchmark demonstrate that Hyper-V2X provides accurate, well-calibrated uncertainty estimates and improves overall perception reliability. Our code and benchmark are publicly available under an open-source license: https://github.com/abhishekjagtap1/Hyper-V2X

AI.tldr: Hyper-V2X is a novel framework using hypernetworks to estimate uncertainties in Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) cooperative perception for autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in uncertainty quantification in V2X-based cooperative perception, despite advancements in multi-agent fusion.
AI.method: Proposed a Bayesian hypernetwork-based system with partial weight generation and V2X context embedding modules to estimate epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties for Bird's-Eye-View segmentation.
AI.result: Hyper-V2X demonstrated accurate and well-calibrated uncertainty estimation with minimal computation overhead on OPV2V benchmarks, enhancing perception reliability.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides an effective, architecture-agnostic solution for improving reliability in cooperative V2X perception, with accessible open-source implementation for further use.

title: Holistic Reliability Propagation: Decoupling Annotation and Prediction for Robust Noisy-Label
summary: Learning with noisy labels in multimedia classification often combines external annotations and model predictions into a single reliability weight, even though the two sources can fail for different reasons. We instead estimate disentangled reliabilities: bilevel meta-learning produces two batch-normalized scalars per sample, alpha for the given label and beta for the pseudo-label, without constraining them to sum to one. Holistic Reliability Propagation (HRP) then routes them to different objectives, using reliability-aware Mixup with global gating on the input branch and beta-gated pseudo-label positives on the contrastive branch. On synthetic and real-world benchmarks, HRP improves average accuracy over strong baselines and remains competitive at the highest noise rates.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel approach for handling noisy labels in multimedia classification by separately estimating reliabilities for annotations and model predictions and leveraging them via a mechanism named Holistic Reliability Propagation (HRP).
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of traditional methods that combine label and prediction reliabilities into a single measure, ignoring their distinct failure reasons.
AI.method: The method uses bilevel meta-learning to estimate separate reliabilities (alpha for labels and beta for pseudo-labels) and employs these in a Holistic Reliability Propagation framework involving reliability-aware Mixup and gated mechanisms.
AI.result: HRP shows improved accuracy on synthetic and real-world benchmarks compared to strong baselines and remains competitive under high noise rates.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling reliabilities and leveraging them differently for multimedia classification enhances robustness and accuracy against noisy labels.

title: Reinforcing Human Behavior Simulation via Verbal Feedback
summary: Humans learn social norms and behaviors from verbal feedback (e.g., a parent saying "that was rude" or a friend explaining "here's why that hurt"). Yet, learning from feedback for LLMs has largely focused on domains like code and math, where RL rewards are directly verifiable and condensed into scalar values. As LLMs are increasingly used to simulate human behavior, e.g., standing in for users, patients, students, and other personas, there is a pressing need to make them more human-like, which requires embracing a fundamentally different kind of signal: feedback that is verbal, subjective, and multi-faceted. We present DITTO, a model trained by treating verbal feedback as a first-class signal in reinforcement learning. After each rollout, DITTO receives verbal feedback and generates a feedback-conditioned improved rollout; both outputs are jointly optimized with GRPO, distilling verbal guidance into the base policy without requiring feedback at test time. We also introduce SOUL (Simulation gym Of hUman-Like behavior), a unified benchmark and training data suite spanning 10 tasks across six categories: Theory of Mind, character role play, social skill, learner simulation, user simulation, and persona simulation. DITTO achieves an average 36% improvement over the base model and exceeds GPT-5.4 on 6 of 10 SOUL benchmarks, demonstrating that RL with verbal feedback is a promising direction for training LLMs to simulate human behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper presents DITTO, a model that uses verbal feedback for reinforcement learning, improving human-like behavior simulation in LLMs and surpassing GPT-5.4 in several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To make LLMs more human-like for applications simulating human behavior, addressing the limitations of current methods that primarily focus on scalar, verifiable rewards.
AI.method: Developed DITTO, a reinforcement learning model that treats verbal feedback as a crucial training signal. Introduced SOUL, a benchmark suite to evaluate human-like behavior in 10 tasks across six categories.
AI.result: DITTO achieved a 36% improvement over its base model and outperformed GPT-5.4 in six out of ten SOUL benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Verbal feedback presents a viable and effective method for enhancing human-like behavior in LLMs, suggesting a shift in training paradigms.

title: Fine-grained Claim-level RAG Benchmark for Law
summary: The rapid progress of large language models (LLMs) is shifting semantic search toward a question-answering paradigm, where users ask questions and LLMs generate responses. In high-stake domains such as law, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is commonly used to mitigate hallucinations in generated responses. Nonetheless, prior work shows that RAG systems, whether general-purpose or legal-specific, still hallucinate at varying rates, making fine-grained evaluation essential. Despite the need, existing evaluation frameworks for legal RAG systems lack the granularity required to provide detailed analysis of retrieval and generation performance separately. Moreover, current benchmarks are largely English-only and centered on legal expert queries, overlooking non-expert needs. We introduce ClaimRAG-LAW, a comprehensive dataset for legal RAG that supports French and English, targets both experts and non-experts, and includes diverse question types reflecting realistic scenarios. We further apply a fine-grained evaluation framework of state-of-the-art legal RAG systems, revealing limitations in retrieval, generation, and claim-level analysis in the legal domain.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ClaimRAG-LAW, a multilingual dataset for legal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), aimed at improving fine-grained evaluations of both expert and non-expert query types.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of granular evaluation frameworks for legal RAG systems, which are critical in mitigating hallucinations in high-stakes domains like law. Existing benchmarks are limited in scope, language support, and inclusivity of non-expert needs.
AI.method: The authors developed the ClaimRAG-LAW dataset that supports English and French, accommodates expert and non-expert users, and incorporates diverse question types. They also applied detailed evaluation metrics for retrieval and generation systems.
AI.result: ClaimRAG-LAW enabled fine-grained performance analysis of leading legal RAG systems, exposing retrieval and generation limitations across various claim levels.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the need for improved multilingual and user-inclusive datasets for legal RAG evaluations, advocating for better tools to minimize issues like hallucinations in question-answering applications.

title: Closed Loop Dynamic Driving Data Mixture for Real-Synthetic Co-Training
summary: Data scaling is fundamental to modern deep learning, and grows increasingly critical as autonomous driving shifts to end-to-end learning. Real-world driving data is expensive to annotate and scene-biased, making real-synthetic co-training with near-infinite synthetic data a promising direction. However, naively incorporating all available synthetic data is inefficient and leads to distribution shifts, and optimizing data mixture under practical training budgets remains a critical yet under-explored problem. In this sense, we claim that the mixture of training data requires clear guidance in terms of scene types and quantities. Particularly in this work, we conceptualize the data mixture approximately as a dynamic optimization process that iteratively adjusts the training data mixture to maximize model performance, guided by closed-loop evaluation feedback, and propose AutoScale, a fully automated closed-loop data engine unifying scene representation, data mixture optimization and retrieval, as well as model training and evaluation. Specifically, we propose Graph Regularized AutoEncoder (Graph-RAE) for driving scene representations, introduce Cluster-aware Gradient Ascent (Cluster-GA) for cluster-wise importance estimation and reweighting, and perform cluster-guided vector retrieval to select high-value samples. Experiments on NavSim demonstrate that AutoScale outperforms vanilla co-training and cross-domain baselines, achieving better performance with fewer synthetic samples under constrained budgets.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AutoScale, an automated framework for optimizing real-synthetic data mixtures in end-to-end autonomous driving systems. It enhances model performance under budget constraints by dynamically adjusting data use through innovative scene representation and data selection methods.
AI.motivation: Modern autonomous driving is increasingly adopting end-to-end learning, which requires extensive training data. Real-world data is expensive and biased, prompting interest in leveraging synthetic data. The challenge lies in efficiently mixing real and synthetic data to maximize performance while avoiding resource waste and distribution shifts.
AI.method: The proposed AutoScale framework consists of a dynamic optimization process integrating three key components: 1) Graph Regularized AutoEncoder (Graph-RAE) for scene representation; 2) Cluster-aware Gradient Ascent (Cluster-GA) for estimating and reweighting cluster-wise sample importance; and 3) a retrieval mechanism to select high-value synthetic samples. Guidance comes from closed-loop evaluation feedback during training.
AI.result: Experiments conducted on NavSim demonstrate that AutoScale surpasses traditional co-training techniques and cross-domain baselines in performance while using fewer synthetic samples within constrained budgets.
AI.conclusion: AutoScale validates the importance of guided data mixture optimization for improving model performance in autonomous driving. The proposed framework serves as a promising method to efficiently co-train real and synthetic data in resource-constrained scenarios.

title: GAMR: Geometric-Aware Manifold Regularization with Virtual Outlier Synthesis for Learning with Noisy Labels
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) experience significant performance degradation when processing noisy labels, primarily due to overfitting on mislabeled data. Current mainstream approaches attempt to mitigate this issue by passively filtering clean samples during training. However, simple sample filtering within feature spaces degraded by noise struggles to distinguish between challenging samples and noisy samples, creating a bottleneck for model performance. We highlight for the first time the fundamental importance of actively reshaping feature space geometry for learning from noisy data. We propose a novel Geometry-aware Manifold Regularization Paradigm whose core idea is to explicitly construct energy barriers between data manifolds by actively synthesizing virtual outlier samples. By imposing geometric constraints that promote intra-class compactness and inter-class separation, this approach enhances the discriminability between hard and noisy samples, leading to the learning of more robust representations. Our regularization mechanism exhibits high universality, with effectiveness independent of any prior assumptions about noise patterns. It can be integrated as a standalone mechanism into existing sample selection frameworks, providing stronger robustness against diverse noisy environments. Experiments demonstrate that our paradigm achieves performance surpassing current state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods on multiple benchmarks, including CIFAR-10, with particularly pronounced advantages under more challenging asymmetric noise conditions. Furthermore, this paradigm significantly enhances the model's capability in Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection, ensuring superior reliability and safety for deployment in open-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of handling noisy labels in deep neural networks by proposing a Geometry-aware Manifold Regularization Paradigm that reshapes feature space to distinguish clean samples more effectively, showcasing state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current methods for handling noisy labels in DNNs passively filter clean samples but struggle due to degraded feature space and an inability to differentiate between hard and noisy samples, affecting model performance.
AI.method: The authors propose a Geometry-aware Manifold Regularization Paradigm that reshapes feature space by constructing energy barriers between data manifolds, synthesizing virtual outlier samples, and imposing geometric constraints to enhance intra-class compactness and inter-class separation.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that the proposed paradigm achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks like CIFAR-10, particularly under challenging asymmetric noise, and improves Out-of-Distribution detection capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method offers a robust and universal regularization approach for noisy label scenarios, significantly enhancing model performance and reliability across diverse environments and tasks.

title: Fast Reconstruction of Exact Maxwell Dynamics from Sparse Data
summary: We introduce FLASH-MAX, a shallow, exact-by-construction neural network architecture for predicting homogeneous electromagnetic fields from sparse pointwise observations. Each hidden neuron represents a separate exact solution to Maxwell's equations, so that the network satisfies the governing equations symbolically by construction and can be trained end-to-end from sparse data within seconds. We prove a universal approximation result showing that this exact model class remains universal on arbitrary domains. FLASH-MAX reaches sub-1% relative validation error from about 1K sparse pointwise observations in seconds, all while maintaining a zero PDE residual, and keeps single-digit errors even for only 100 observations sampled from 3D space. These results suggest that moving governing structure from the loss into the hypothesis class can dramatically improve the trade-off between precision and optimization speed in scientific machine learning.

AI.tldr: FLASH-MAX is a neural network architecture designed to predict electromagnetic fields from sparse observations while exactly satisfying Maxwell's equations.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of predicting homogeneous electromagnetic fields efficiently and accurately from sparse pointwise data.
AI.method: The architecture encodes exact symbolic solutions to Maxwell's equations into the neural network structure, ensuring compliance with these equations by construction. It enables fast end-to-end training.
AI.result: FLASH-MAX can achieve below 1% relative validation error with approximately 1,000 observations in seconds and maintains low errors even with only 100 data points.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating governing equations directly into the network's hypothesis class improves both precision and optimization speed in scientific machine learning.

title: GradeLegal: Automated Grading for German Legal Cases
summary: Grading German legal exam solutions faces growing volumes and a shortage of qualified graders, delaying feedback and creating a bottleneck. At the same time, it is a high-stakes expert task, since state exam grades strongly influence career outcomes in Germany. Despite this practical relevance, literature lacks systematic studies on effective methods for grading legal exams. To address this gap, we investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can support the automated grading of German legal case solutions in criminal and public law, thereby enabling scalable feedback and student self-testing. We present a systematic evaluation of 27 proprietary and open-source LLMs, benchmarking prompting strategies that incrementally add task-related information, such as a sample solution and a grading rubric. Using quadratic weighted kappa (QWK), reasoning-oriented LLMs can approximate expert grading in public law when given a sample solution and a grading rubric (up to 0.91), compared to 0.60 in criminal law, suggesting a harder grading task in criminal law. Beyond single-model grading, ensembling improves agreement by up to 0.15 over its best member and can offer an alternative to stronger closed-source single models. In addition, our findings suggest that effective prompt design and model selection are necessary for reliable LLM-based grading of legal exams.

AI.tldr: The study investigates the use of large language models (LLMs) for automating grading in German legal exams, revealing that reasoning-oriented LLMs can approximate expert grading in public law, though criminal law grading is more challenging.
AI.motivation: The growing volume of legal exam solutions in Germany, coupled with a shortage of qualified graders and the high stakes of state exam grades, necessitates a scalable and efficient grading solution.
AI.method: The study systematically evaluated 27 LLMs (both proprietary and open-source) using different prompting strategies, such as incorporating sample solutions and grading rubrics. Effectiveness was measured using quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) to assess agreement with expert grading.
AI.result: Reasoning-oriented LLMs achieved a QWK of up to 0.91 in public law when provided with sample solutions and grading rubrics, while performance in criminal law was lower (0.60), indicating a more challenging domain. Ensemble methods improved performance by up to 0.15 over the best LLM.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can facilitate scalable and reliable grading in legal exams, particularly in public law, though challenges remain for criminal law. Effective prompt design and model selection are crucial for success.

title: Early High-Frequency Injection for Geometry-Sensitive OOD Detection
summary: Post-hoc OOD detectors score logits or features after training, so their success depends on the geometry already encoded in the representation. We revisit this assumption through a band-wise MMD^2 analysis across CE, SimCLR, SupCon, and the OOD-oriented representation method PALM. In our diagnostic, low-frequency input bands induce weaker ID/OOD feature discrepancy, whereas higher-frequency bands tend to provide stronger separability. This observation motivates EIHF, an input-side intervention that exposes high-frequency evidence before the first convolution without changing the training objective. EIHF is strongest for geometry-sensitive OOD detection: under matched training and scoring settings, it reshapes class-conditional feature geometry and reduces ID/OOD Mahalanobis score overlap. Experiments on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 show gains on CIFAR-100 and the best average FPR95 with second-best average AUROC on ImageNet-100, while also revealing a limitation on the scene-centric Places shift. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/EIHF.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates out-of-distribution (OOD) detection through input frequency analysis, proposing an intervention called EIHF to enhance OOD separability.
AI.motivation: The success of post-hoc OOD detection relies on the geometry encoded in feature representations, which varies across different input frequency bands.
AI.method: The paper uses a band-wise MMD^2 analysis to study input frequency effects on feature discrepancy and introduces EIHF, a method that emphasizes high-frequency evidence before the first convolution during model training.
AI.result: EIHF reshapes feature geometry for effective OOD detection, yielding improved metrics like Mahalanobis score separability and performance gains on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 datasets.
AI.conclusion: EIHF enhances OOD detection, particularly in geometry-sensitive scenarios, providing competitive performance on various benchmarks but showing limitations on some scene-centric datasets like Places.

title: Online Conformal Prediction with Corrupted Feedback
summary: Modern artificial intelligence systems require calibrated uncertainty estimates that remain reliable in sequential and non-stationary environments. Online conformal prediction (OCP) addresses this challenge through adaptively updated prediction sets that provide deterministic long-run miscoverage guarantees. These guarantees, however, hinge on the assumption of perfect feedback about the coverage of past prediction sets. In practice, the observed miscoverage indicator may be corrupted by noise, communication failures, or adversarial manipulation, which can severely degrade OCP's calibration guarantees. In this paper, we study OCP under corrupted feedback. We first model feedback corruption as an arbitrary binary flip sequence, and analyze how feedback corruption affects and degrades the miscoverage performance of standard OCP. We then propose two robust schemes: robust OCP via filtering, which leverages the structural properties of the predicted threshold to filter corrupted feedback, and robust OCP via active compensation, which incorporates an active compensation mechanism to mitigate the effect of corrupted feedback. For both methods, we establish explicit miscoverage guarantees, which are further specialized for an independent stochastic flip model and for an arbitrary error model with memory bounds. Experiments on real-world datasets validate the proposed approach, showing markedly improved calibration and significantly smaller prediction sets compared with baseline OCP methods under corrupted feedback.

AI.tldr: The paper tackles challenges in Online Conformal Prediction (OCP) under corrupted feedback scenarios and introduces robust methods to ensure improved performance and calibration.
AI.motivation: To address the issue of maintaining reliable uncertainty estimates in modern AI systems, especially under sequential and non-stationary environments where feedback can be corrupted.
AI.method: The authors model corrupted feedback as a binary sequence and propose two robust methods: filtering and active compensation, analyzing their miscoverage guarantees under different corruption models.
AI.result: Explicit miscoverage guarantees are provided for the proposed methods. Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate improved calibration and reduced prediction set sizes under corrupted feedback compared to standard OCP methods.
AI.conclusion: Robust OCP approaches leveraging feedback filtering and compensation can significantly enhance calibration and performance in adversarial or noisy feedback environments.

title: LoCar: Localization-Aware Evaluation of In-Vehicle Assistants through Fine-Grained Sociolinguistic Control
comment: To appear in ACL 2026 Industry Track
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into in-vehicle conversational systems, identifying the optimal model remains challenging due to the lack of domain-specific evaluation standards tailored to real-world deployment requirements. In this paper, we propose a novel evaluation framework for in-vehicle assistants, with a particular focus on Korean-language localization. Our empirical analysis reveals notable patterns in model behavior. First, fine-grained Korean honorific control remains unstable in current LLMs, indicating that precise speech-level realization must be explicitly evaluated in localization settings. Second, models exhibit weaker performance in strategic conversational metrics like clarification and proactivity. Our analysis suggests this stems from the inherent subjective complexity of these tasks, where our framework adopts a conservative evaluation stance to prioritize reliability. Together, our findings underscore that automotive AI must move beyond general competence toward precise linguistic tailoring and reliable, safety-oriented interaction management.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel evaluation framework for in-vehicle assistants with a focus on Korean-language localization, identifying challenges like honorific control and conversational metrics.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the lack of domain-specific evaluation standards for LLMs in automotive conversational systems, particularly those meeting real-world requirements.
AI.method: The paper proposes a targeted evaluation framework that analyzes key conversational metrics such as Korean honorific control and interaction quality using empirical methods.
AI.result: The study highlights unstable Korean honorific control in LLMs and weaker performance in conversational metrics like clarification and proactivity due to task complexity.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest the need for automotive AI to focus on precise linguistic adaptability and safety-oriented interaction, going beyond general language modeling capabilities.

title: Fully Actuated Manifold Constraint Based Output Feedback Control for Input-Constrained Uncertain Nonlinear Systems
summary: This paper presents a low-complexity, model-free, output-feedback controller for a class of unknown time-varying nonlinear systems with unknown input constraints. The controller achieves the preset control accuracy when the actuator is not saturated and maintains flexible control accuracy after actuator saturation. This result extends existing constraint control methods for linear manifolds to a more general form, including the construction of nonlinear manifolds and various types of constraints, thereby achieving preset control accuracy within finite or fixed time. Additionally, flexible control under unknown saturation is achieved through the construction of an error-driven flexible constraint. Finally, second-order and higher-order control examples and simulations are provided.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a low-complexity, model-free output-feedback controller that handles unknown time-varying nonlinear systems with unknown input constraints, delivering preset control accuracy under various conditions.
AI.motivation: To develop a controller for nonlinear systems that can handle unknown time-varying behavior and unknown input constraints while maintaining or adjusting control accuracy even under actuator saturation.
AI.method: The proposed method extends existing linear-manifold constraint control techniques to nonlinear manifolds, incorporating error-driven flexible constraints to achieve preset or adjustable control accuracy.
AI.result: The controller achieves preset accuracy within finite or fixed time when unsaturated, and flexible control accuracy is maintained under unknown actuator saturation.
AI.conclusion: The method broadens the scope of constraint control, enhances flexibility in nonlinear systems, and is demonstrated with second-order and higher-order simulations.

title: TASTE: A Designer-Annotated Multi-Dimensional Preference Dataset for AI-Generated Graphic Design
summary: Text-to-image models produce graphic design at production scale, but their supervision comes from photo-style preference data with a single overall verdict per comparison. Designers evaluate along several distinct axes, including typography, visual hierarchy, color harmony, layout, and brief fidelity, and a single label collapses them. We release TASTE (Typography, Aesthetics, Spatial, Tone, Etc.): ten professional designers ranked outputs from four current text-to-image models on nine criteria across two disjoint cohorts, yielding 1,600 ratings per criterion plus per-image hallucination flags on the holistic-preference cohorts. We pair the dataset with three contributions. First, a criterion-agnostic signal test framework, using Kendall's tau, majority probability, and Condorcet cycles against exact iid-uniform nulls at p = 4 and R = 5, places designer agreement on graphic design between food and movie preferences and photo-style image quality, with every TASTE criterion rejecting the random-rater null. Second, no pre-trained system in our benchmark, including six open-weight VLM judges from 3B to 33B parameters and three dedicated T2I scorers, HPSv2.1, PickScore-v1, and LAION-Aesthetic-V2, exceeds 0.55 macro agreement with the 5-designer majority; VLM judges trade off position bias against content sensitivity, so scaling moves along this frontier without improving accuracy. Third, a small pairwise-difference head trained on TASTE reaches 0.611, closing roughly half the gap to the 0.741 single-rater ceiling.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TASTE, a dataset created by professional graphic designers to evaluate text-to-image models across multiple criteria, alongside analytic frameworks and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-image models are evaluated using general photo-style preferences, which fail to account for the nuanced axes of graphic design assessment that professional designers use.
AI.method: The authors collected evaluations from professional designers on nine specific design criteria, created a signal test framework to assess agreement on these criteria, and benchmarked pre-trained models against human judgments.
AI.result: The dataset established that designer agreement exceeds random chance but is less than with other subjective domains. No pre-trained system surpasses 0.55 macro agreement with human judgment. A fine-tuned model trained on the TASTE dataset improved agreement to 0.611.
AI.conclusion: TASTE fills a gap in granular aesthetic evaluation by providing multi-criteria design ratings, demonstrating the limits of current models and the potential for improvement through tailored fine-tuning.

title: An exponential mechanism based on quadratic approximations for fine-tuning machine learning models with privacy guarantees
summary: Fine-tuning adapts a pretrained machine learning model to a small, sensitive dataset, but this process risks memorizing individual new data points, making the model vulnerable to adversaries who seek to extract sensitive information. In this work, we develop a randomized algorithm based on the exponential mechanism for fine-tuning while ensuring differential privacy. Our key idea is to construct a simple utility function that combines a local quadratic approximation of the pretrained model with information from the new dataset. The resulting exponential mechanism admits exact sampling from a multivariate normal distribution in closed form. We establish theoretical privacy guarantees, sensitivity bounds, and accuracy estimations for our method. We further introduce a random-projection strategy that makes the approach scalable to high-dimensional models. Numerical experiments on the MNIST benchmark and the MIMIC clinical dataset demonstrate competitive performance against existing differentially private fine-tuning techniques.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a randomized algorithm for differentially private fine-tuning of pretrained models, using the exponential mechanism and a scalable random-projection strategy.
AI.motivation: To address the risk of pretrained models memorizing sensitive data during fine-tuning, which makes them vulnerable to adversarial attacks.
AI.method: A differentially private fine-tuning approach based on the exponential mechanism with a local quadratic utility and a random-projection strategy for scalable application.
AI.result: The approach achieves competitive privacy and accuracy performance on MNIST and MIMIC datasets compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method balances data privacy and model performance effectively, making it a viable solution for sensitive datasets.

title: WCXB: A Multi-Type Web Content Extraction Benchmark
comment: Dataset: github.com/Murrough-Foley/web-content-extraction-benchmark, doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19316874. Leaderboard: webcontentextraction.org. Preprint also deposited at doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19664685
summary: Web content extraction - isolating a page's main content from surrounding boilerplate - is a prerequisite for search indexing, retrieval-augmented generation, NLP dataset construction, and large language model training. Progress in this area has been constrained by the limitations of existing evaluation benchmarks, which are small (100-800 pages), restricted to news articles, or based on web pages from over a decade ago. We introduce the Web Content Extraction Benchmark (WCXB), a dataset of 2,008 web pages from 1,613 domains spanning seven structurally distinct page types: articles, forums, products, collections, listings, documentation, and service pages. The dataset includes a 1,497-page development set and a 511-page held-out test set with matched page type distributions. Ground truth annotations were produced through a five-stage pipeline: LLM-assisted drafting, automated verification, four-pass frontier model review, snippet and quality verification scripts, and human review. We evaluate 13 extraction systems - 11 heuristic and 2 neural - and find that while top systems converge on articles (F1 = 0.93), performance diverges sharply on structured page types (F1 = 0.41-0.84), revealing blind spots invisible to existing article-only benchmarks. The dataset is released under CC-BY-4.0 with HTML source files, ground truth annotations, page type labels, and baseline results.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new benchmark, WCXB, for web content extraction using a diverse dataset of 2,008 web pages and demonstrates challenges in current extraction systems, especially with non-article page types.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for web content extraction are limited in size, scope, and relevance to modern web pages, hindering progress in search indexing, NLP tasks, and language model training.
AI.method: The authors created the Web Content Extraction Benchmark (WCXB), featuring 2,008 pages across seven distinct types, and used a comprehensive pipeline involving LLMs, verification tools, and human review to generate ground truth annotations.
AI.result: The benchmark evaluation of 13 extraction systems shows strong performance on articles (F1 = 0.93) but significant issues with structured page types (F1 = 0.41-0.84), highlighting current system limitations.
AI.conclusion: WCXB provides a comprehensive and modern dataset for web content extraction evaluation and reveals critical areas where existing systems underperform, encouraging broader capabilities beyond article extraction.

title: Deep Attention Reweighting: Post-Hoc Attention-Based Feature Aggregation in CNNs for Disentangling Core and Spurious Features under Spurious Correlations
summary: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) often exploit spurious correlations in datasets, learning superficially predictive yet causally irrelevant features, leading to poor generalization and fairness issues. Deep Feature Reweighting (DFR) is a post-hoc technique that reduces a trained model's reliance on spurious correlations by retraining its classification head on a target dataset. However, we show that DFR is fundamentally constrained by operating on entangled features, limiting its ability to amplify the core features while simultaneously suppressing the spurious ones. We trace this entanglement to the ubiquitous Global Average Pooling (GAP) layer, which indiscriminately collapses spatially distinct core and spurious features into a single representation. To address this, we propose Deep Attention Reweighting (DAR), a post-hoc attention-based aggregation module that replaces GAP and is retrained jointly with the classification head. DAR computes an adaptive weighting of spatial locations across feature maps, enabling selective suppression of spurious features before the collapse into entangled features. Across various datasets, metrics, and ablations, DAR consistently outperforms DFR, demonstrating that our attention-based aggregation mitigates GAP-induced entanglement and reduces spurious reliance.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies limitations in the Deep Feature Reweighting (DFR) method for mitigating spurious correlations in Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and proposes Deep Attention Reweighting (DAR) as a superior alternative.
AI.motivation: CNNs often learn spurious correlations from datasets, harming generalization and fairness. The current method, DFR, faces limitations due to feature entanglement caused by the Global Average Pooling (GAP) layer. This motivates the need for a method that better separates core and spurious features.
AI.method: The authors introduce DAR, an attention-based aggregation module that replaces GAP in a post-hoc manner. DAR adaptively weighs spatial locations across feature maps, selectively suppressing spurious features before they become entangled with core features. It is retrained alongside the classification head.
AI.result: Experiments show that DAR outperforms DFR across diverse datasets and metrics, effectively reducing reliance on spurious correlations and mitigating GAP-induced entanglement.
AI.conclusion: DAR is a robust method for attenuating spurious correlations in CNNs by resolving the limitations of GAP and surpassing the efficacy of DFR, improving generalization and fairness.

title: Machine-Learning-Enhanced Non-Invasive Testing for MASLD Fibrosis: Shallow-Deep Neural Networks Versus FIB-4, Tabular Foundation Models, and Large Language Models
summary:
Advanced fibrosis is a major determinant of liver-related morbidity in metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD). FIB-4 is widely used as a first-line non-invasive test, but its fixed formula may underuse diagnostic information contained in age, aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, and platelet count. We evaluated whether machine-learning-enhanced non-invasive testing (MLE-NIT) can improve advanced fibrosis detection while preserving this FIB-4 variable space.
  We used three biopsy-confirmed MASLD cohorts from China, Malaysia, and India (n=784). The Chinese cohort was split into 486 training and 54 internal validation/tuning patients; final performance was reported only on the Malaysian and Indian external cohorts. Models used five variables: age, FIB-4, aspartate aminotransferase, platelet count, and alanine aminotransferase. We compared FIB-4 with a shallow-deep neural network (s-DNN), TabPFN, and gpt-4o-2024-08-06.
  FIB-4 achieved external ROC-AUCs of 0.75 and 0.60 in Malaysia and India, respectively. TabPFN achieved 0.69 and 0.66, fine-tuned GPT-4o achieved 0.75 and 0.63, and the s-DNN achieved 0.77 and 0.67, respectively. The s-DNN contained only 354 trainable parameters, compared with 7,244,554 for TabPFN, yet provided a more balanced external operating profile. Calibration showed s-DNN Brier scores of 0.18 and 0.22, and permutation importance identified AST and FIB-4 as dominant variables. Compact non-linear MLE-NITs may enhance FIB-4-based fibrosis assessment without increasing clinical data requirements.

AI.tldr: The study explores machine-learning-enhanced non-invasive testing (MLE-NIT) to improve advanced fibrosis detection in MASLD, focusing on optimizing the diagnostic potential of FIB-4 variables without demanding additional clinical data.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of the fixed FIB-4 formula in fully utilizing diagnostic information from its variables, with the aim of improving advanced fibrosis detection in MASLD.
AI.method: The study utilized three MASLD cohorts from China, Malaysia, and India. Machine-learning models, including shallow-deep neural network (s-DNN), TabPFN, and fine-tuned GPT-4o, were trained and tested against FIB-4 using age, AST, ALT, and platelet count as inputs.
AI.result: The s-DNN outperformed other models in external ROC-AUC scores, achieving 0.77 in Malaysia and 0.67 in India, with superior calibration and fewer trainable parameters compared to TabPFN and GPT-4o.
AI.conclusion: Compact non-linear machine-learning models like s-DNN can potentially optimize the FIB-4 framework for fibrosis detection while maintaining low complexity and not requiring additional clinical inputs.

title: Sketch2MinSurf: Vision-Language Guided Generation of Editable Minimal Surfaces from Hand-Drawn Sketches
summary: Converting hand-drawn sketches into structured 3D geometries remains challenging due to the difficulty of representing non-Euclidean surfaces and maintaining topological consistency. Existing generative models such as GANs, NeRFs, and diffusion architectures often fail to produce editable manifolds directly usable in downstream design workflows. We present Sketch2MinSurf, a hybrid vision-language and geometric optimization framework that integrates vision-language guidance with minimal-surface theory to generate smooth and editable 3D surfaces from hand-drawn sketches. The core of our approach is a spatial-topological encoding that represents geometry as tuples of node coordinates and real/virtual edge skeletons, enabling stable topological control during generation. We further introduce the Sketch2MinSurf Structural Loss (S2MS-Loss), a reward-modulated objective that jointly constrains geometric reconstruction and topological coherence. On a test set of 100 sketches, Sketch2MinSurf achieves a topological similarity score of 0.844, outperforming existing sketch-to-shape baselines. The generated manifolds are directly editable and free from non-manifold artifacts. A public art installation at a university showcases the method's potential for human-intent-driven 3D form generation. The dataset and code are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Sketch2MinSurf/.

AI.tldr: Sketch2MinSurf converts hand-drawn sketches into smooth, editable 3D geometries using a hybrid vision-language and geometric optimization framework.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenges in converting hand-drawn sketches to structured 3D geometries, such as handling non-Euclidean surfaces and achieving topological consistency, areas where existing generative models fall short.
AI.method: Sketch2MinSurf integrates vision-language guidance with minimal-surface theory, using a spatial-topological encoding for geometry representation and introducing the Sketch2MinSurf Structural Loss (S2MS-Loss) for balancing geometric reconstruction and topological coherence.
AI.result: Sketch2MinSurf achieves a topological similarity score of 0.844 on 100 test sketches, outperforming existing methods and producing 3D manifolds that are editable and free of non-manifold artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The method demonstrates strong capabilities in converting sketches to 3D forms suitable for real-world applications, with a public art installation validating its effectiveness. The code and dataset are publicly available for further use and exploration.

title: Ada2MS: A Hybrid Optimization Algorithm Based on Exponential Mixing of Elementwise and Global Second-Moment Estimates
summary: Optimization algorithms are core methods by which machine learning models iteratively minimize loss functions, update parameters, learn from data, and improve performance. Momentum SGD and AdamW represent two important optimization paradigms. AdamW produces stable updates and usually has strong robustness across training scenarios, but its generalization performance is sometimes weaker than that of momentum methods. Momentum SGD can often obtain better generalization after careful tuning, but it is more sensitive to gradient-scale variation and hyperparameter settings. To balance the strengths and weaknesses of the two paradigms, this paper proposes Ada2MS, an optimization algorithm that achieves a smooth transition between AdamW-like behavior and momentum-SGD-like behavior through continuous exponential interpolation between elementwise second-moment estimates and global second-moment estimates. On the visual tasks evaluated in this study, Ada2MS obtains competitive results under a unified optimizer-comparison protocol. The code will be released at https://github.com/mengzhu0308/Ada2MS

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Ada2MS, a new optimization algorithm combining features of AdamW and Momentum SGD for enhanced generalization and robustness in visual tasks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the trade-offs between AdamW's stability and robustness and Momentum SGD's potential for better generalization but higher sensitivity to hyperparameters.
AI.method: A novel algorithm called Ada2MS was developed, enabling a smooth transition between AdamW-like and Momentum SGD-like behaviors using exponential interpolation of second-moment estimates.
AI.result: Ada2MS demonstrated competitive performance under a unified optimizer-comparison protocol in visual tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Ada2MS effectively balances the strengths of two major optimization paradigms and shows promising outcomes for machine learning.

title: Smarter edits? Post-editing with error highlights and translation suggestions
summary: As MT quality increases, interest in enhanced post-editing features such as QE-derived error highlights is growing, yet evidence for their usefulness remains limited. In this work, we explore the usefulness of LLM-derived error highlights and correction suggestions based on automatic post-editing (APE). We conduct a study where professional translators (En-Nl) post-edit translations using APE error highlights and correction suggestions and compare productivity, quality and user experience to regular PE and PE with QE-derived highlights. While no condition yielded productivity or quality gains compared to regular PE, APE highlights were better received than QE-derived highlights, and correction suggestions improved overall user experience.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates the usefulness of features like APE-derived error highlights in translation post-editing. They find no significant productivity or quality gains but note better reception of APE highlights and improved user experience.
AI.motivation: To explore whether enhanced post-editing features, such as AI-derived error highlighting and correction suggestions, can improve translator productivity and experience.
AI.method: The study involved professional English-to-Dutch translators post-editing translations under three conditions: regular post-editing, post-editing with QE-derived highlights, and post-editing with APE-derived error highlights and corrections.
AI.result: Although no improvements in productivity or quality were observed, APE-derived highlights were preferred over QE-derived ones, and correction suggestions boosted user experiences.
AI.conclusion: While enhanced features like APE-derived highlights and corrections don't yet improve performance metrics, they positively impact translator experience and warrant further exploration.

title: Lowering the Barrier to IREX Participation: Open-Source Algorithms, Toolkit, and Benchmarking for Iris Recognition
summary: This paper proposes two new open-source iris recognition algorithms, providing both Python and IREX-compliant C++ implementations to be submitted to the official IREX X program. This work has two primary goals: (a) to conduct the first-ever assessment of open-source iris recognition solutions according to IREX testing protocols, and (b) to offer a model C++ submission that significantly facilitates the entry of other teams' open-source methods into the IREX evaluation. The new methods consist of two Neural Networks trained with: (i) Triplet loss with Batch-Hard Triplet mining (TripletIris), and (ii) ArcFace loss (ArcIris). The paper also provides open-source IREX-compliant C++ implementations of two existing methods: (a) an iris image filtering-based algorithm utilizing human saliency-driven kernels (HDBIF), and (b) a human-interpretable algorithm for detecting and comparing Fuchs' crypts (CRYPTS). Except for CRYPTS, which faced timing constraints during 1:N search, these methods have undergone the official IREX X evaluation and have also been assessed using several popular academic benchmarks: Quality-Face/Iris Research Ensemble, Warsaw-Biobase Post-Mortem Iris, CASIA-Iris-Thousand-V4, CASIA-Iris-Lamp-V4, IIT Delhi Iris Database, IIITD Contact Lens Iris Database, NDIris3D, and Notre Dame Variable Iris Image Quality Release 2. Finally, this paper also provides open-source models for iris segmentation and circle estimation that can be incorporated into any new iris recognition method.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces two new open-source iris recognition algorithms with Python and IREX-compliant C++ implementations, alongside assessments and resources to support open-source contributions to IREX evaluation.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to enable the evaluation of open-source iris recognition solutions using standardized IREX protocols and to simplify the integration and submission of open-source methods for future evaluations.
AI.method: The authors propose two new iris recognition methods: one trained using Triplet loss with Batch-Hard Triplet mining (TripletIris) and the other using ArcFace loss (ArcIris). Open-source implementations of existing methods, HDBIF and CRYPTS, are also provided alongside resources for iris segmentation and circle estimation.
AI.result: The proposed and existing methods have undergone IREX X evaluation (except CRYPTS), and performance has been tested on multiple academic iris recognition datasets. Results were published to enhance usability and reproducibility in the domain.
AI.conclusion: This work paves the way for greater participation of open-source solutions in IREX evaluations and provides valuable tools and benchmarks for the iris recognition community to advance research and innovation.

title: Automated ICD Classification of Psychiatric Diagnoses: From Classical NLP to Large Language Models
summary: Mental health has become a global priority, leading to a massive administrative burden in the coding of clinical diagnoses. This study proposes the automation of psychiatric diagnostic analysis by mapping free-text descriptions to the International Classification of Diseases (ICD) using Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Machine Learning (ML) techniques. Utilizing a specialized dataset of 145,513 Spanish psychiatric descriptions, various text representation paradigms were evaluated, ranging from classical frequency-based models (BoW, TF-IDF) to state-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs) such as e5\_large, BioLORD, and Llama-3-8B. Results indicate that transformer-based embeddings consistently outperform traditional methods by capturing implicit semantic cues and nuanced medical terminology. The e5\_large model, through end-to-end fine-tuning, achieved the highest performance with a $F1_{micro}$ score of 0.866. This research demonstrates that adapting LLMs to specific clinical nomenclature is essential for overcoming the challenges of ``long-tail'' label distributions and the inherent ambiguity of psychiatric discourse.

AI.tldr: This study automates psychiatric diagnostic coding to ICD using NLP and ML methods, with transformer models achieving superior accuracy.
AI.motivation: The administrative burden associated with mental health diagnostic coding necessitates efficient automated solutions.
AI.method: This study utilized 145,513 Spanish psychiatric descriptions and tested text representation methods, including LLMs and traditional models, with fine-tuned e5_large delivering best results.
AI.result: The e5_large model achieved the highest micro-average F1 score of 0.866, outperforming classical approaches.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuned LLMs are vital for resolving challenges in psychiatric diagnostic automation, such as label distribution issues and semantics ambiguity.

title: Resolving Long-Tail Ambiguity in Unsupervised 3D Point Cloud Segmentation with Language Priors
comment: In submission. The code will be released at: https://github.com/Whisky0129/langtail_official
summary: Existing approaches for unsupervised 3D point cloud segmentation predominantly rely on a purely visual similarity-based learning-by-clustering paradigm, which suffers from a fundamental limitation: long-tail ambiguity. In such a paradigm, features of minor classes are consistently absorbed by dominant clusters, leading to severely imbalanced predictions. To address this issue, we propose LangTail, a language-guided hierarchical learning framework that leverages the balanced world knowledge encoded in language models to mitigate long-tail ambiguity in unsupervised 3D segmentation. The key idea is to establish multi-level associations between language-derived semantic priors and visually underrepresented minor classes, thereby compensating for the biased attention of purely visual clustering toward dominant classes. Specifically, LangTail first constructs an entity-level semantic prior from language models, capturing balanced and fine-grained world knowledge across categories. These priors are injected into a hierarchical clustering framework via contrastive alignment. This guides multi-granularity semantic structure formation and prevents minor classes from being absorbed by dominant clusters, yielding more discriminative representations for underrepresented categories. Extensive experiments on ScanNet-v2, S3DIS, and nuScenes demonstrate that LangTail consistently outperforms existing methods by significant margins, \ie, +13.5, +12.9, and +8.9 mIoU, respectively. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of language priors in improving the representation of minority classes in 3D point clouds. The code will be released at: https://github.com/Whisky0129/langtail_official.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LangTail, a framework addressing the long-tail ambiguity issue in unsupervised 3D point cloud segmentation using semantic priors from language models, achieving significant performance gains in benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Long-tail ambiguity in unsupervised 3D segmentation causes minor class features to be absorbed by dominant clusters, leading to imbalanced predictions.
AI.method: The LangTail framework leverages a hierarchical learning approach by incorporating language-derived semantic priors, aligning them with visual clustering via contrastive alignment to mitigate bias against minor classes.
AI.result: LangTail outperforms existing methods in 3D segmentation benchmarks such as ScanNet-v2 (+13.5 mIoU), S3DIS (+12.9 mIoU), and nuScenes (+8.9 mIoU), demonstrating improved representation for underrepresented categories.
AI.conclusion: LangTail effectively mitigates the imbalance in unsupervised 3D point cloud segmentation by utilizing balanced world knowledge from language models, improving the identification of minority classes.

title: OpenSeisML: Open Large-Scale Real Seismic and well-log Dataset for Generative AI
summary: The advent of machine learning (ML) and computer vision has significantly accelerated seismic inversion workflows by reducing the computational cost of traditionally expensive iterative methods. However, the development and evaluation of ML methods remain limited by the scarcity of realistic velocity models, as most high-quality data are privately owned by oil and gas companies. To address this gap, we present OpenSeisML, a collection of real seismic datasets designed to support generative AI (Gen-AI) workflows for seismic inversion. The datasets are curated from publicly available surveys in the UK National Data Repository (NDR). When seismic volumes are in the time domain and wells are in depth, a time-to-depth conversion is required. We use checkshot data to establish the time-depth relationship and construct a velocity model through interpolation for accurate conversion of post-stack seismic data. Here, we present an automated data curation pipeline that enables seismic data preparation while ensuring reproducibility. The objective is to train a generative model that captures the statistical distribution of subsurface properties, enabling the synthesis of multiple statistically consistent realizations for uncertainty quantification which can act as a prior for seismic inversion.

AI.tldr: OpenSeisML introduces publicly available seismic datasets curated from the UK National Data Repository to address the lack of realistic velocity models for machine learning in seismic inversion.
AI.motivation: To overcome the scarcity of publicly accessible, high-quality velocity models, which limits the development of ML methods for seismic inversion.
AI.method: The authors curate real seismic datasets using publicly available surveys and employ an automated pipeline for time-to-depth conversion and velocity model construction.
AI.result: The development of an automated data curation pipeline for reproducible seismic data preparation, enabling ML training and generative AI workflows.
AI.conclusion: OpenSeisML facilitates uncertainty quantification and acts as a prior for seismic inversion by generating statistically consistent subsurface realizations.

title: Metaphors in Literary Post-Editing: Opening Pandora's Box?
comment: This paper has been accepted for presentation at the EAMT Conference 2026, which will take place in Tilburg from June 15 to 18, 2026
summary: This paper investigates how post-editors of literary texts react and respond to the way metaphors have been translated by Neu ral Machine Translation (NMT) and Large Language Models (LLMs). The results show that one in three metaphors in the output were changed by the post-editors, demonstrating that the translation of fig urative language is indeed problematic in literary MT (LitMT). The responses indi cate that the post-editors were aware of overly literal translations, though mostly for multiword expressions. Moreover, at times they found it difficult to determine whether solutions were acceptable. They rated the overall quality of the MT out put as quite poor and stated that the post editing was more work and more effort than it would have been translating from scratch. This supports previous studies ar guing that post-editing constrains transla tors in their creativity and diminishes their sense of text ownership.

AI.tldr: The paper examines post-editors’ reactions to metaphor translations by NMT and LLMs in literary texts, showing significant challenges and effort required.
AI.motivation: To investigate the challenges in translating metaphors via NMT and LLMs in literary texts and how post-editors respond to them.
AI.method: Analyzed how post-editors modify metaphor translations from NMT and LLM outputs, and gathered their qualitative feedback on the process.
AI.result: One in three translated metaphors were changed by post-editors, who rated the translations poorly and felt constrained in creativity, preferring original translation over post-editing.
AI.conclusion: Post-editing metaphor translations in literary texts is problematic, involves high effort, and impacts translators’ creativity and ownership negatively, questioning the efficacy of NMT and LLMs for literary translation.

title: STAR-IOD: Scale-decoupled Topology Alignment with Pseudo-label Refinement for Remote Sensing Incremental Object Detection
comment: STAR-IOD was accepted by ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
summary: Remote sensing imagery typically arrives in the form of continuous data streams. Traditional detectors often forget previously learned categories when learning new ones; therefore, research on Remote Sensing Incremental Object Detection (RS-IOD) is of great significance. However, existing methods largely overlook the intra-class scale variations prevalent in remote sensing scenes, which undermines the effectiveness of knowledge transfer and old knowledge preservation. Moreover, RS-IOD also suffers from missing annotations, which cause the model to misclassify old-class instances as background. To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework, STAR-IOD. First, we introduce a Subspace-decoupled Topology Distillation (STD) module to transfer structural knowledge, explicitly aligning inter-class topological relationships and mitigating intra-class representation discrepancies induced by scale shifts. Furthermore, we introduce the Clustering-driven Pseudo-label Generator (CPG), a plug-and-play module that leverages K-Means clustering to dynamically identify class-specific thresholds, thereby guaranteeing an accurate distinction between true positive targets and background noise and alleviating the issue of missing annotations for old classes. We also constructed two Remote Sensing Incremental Object Detection datasets, DIOR-IOD and DOTA-IOD to facilitate research on RS-IOD. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches by 1.7% and 2.1% mAP on DIOR-IOD and DOTA-IOD, respectively, effectively alleviating catastrophic forgetting while preserving strong detection performance on both base and novel classes. The code and dataset are released at: https://github.com/zyt95579/STAR-IOD.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes STAR-IOD, a novel framework to address challenges in Remote Sensing Incremental Object Detection (RS-IOD) caused by intra-class scale variations and missing annotations.
AI.motivation: Traditional object detectors struggle with catastrophic forgetting when learning new categories in RS-IOD, compounded by scale variations and annotation gaps, necessitating a more robust solution.
AI.method: The method introduces a Subspace-decoupled Topology Distillation (STD) module for structural knowledge transfer and a Clustering-driven Pseudo-label Generator (CPG) to address missing annotations using K-Means clustering.
AI.result: Experiments show STAR-IOD surpasses state-of-the-art RS-IOD methods by 1.7% and 2.1% mAP on the newly constructed DIOR-IOD and DOTA-IOD datasets, preserving detection performance on both base and novel classes.
AI.conclusion: STAR-IOD effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting and improves performance in remote sensing applications, offering a significant step forward in RS-IOD research with open-source resources available for further study.

title: Manga109-v2026: Revisiting Manga109 Annotations for Modern Manga Understanding
comment: Accepted to the Culture x AI Workshop at ICML 2026. Project page: https://manga109.github.io/manga109-project-website/en/
summary: Manga is a culturally distinctive multimodal medium and one of the most influential forms of Japanese popular culture. As AI systems increasingly target manga understanding, OCR, and translation, Manga109 has become a foundational dataset for manga-related AI research. However, the current Manga109 dataset contains transcription errors and coarse annotations, which do not align well with modern OCR and multimodal manga understanding tasks. In this work, we revisit the dialogue text annotations of Manga109 and identify five categories of annotation issues, including transcription errors, missing text regions, overlapping dialogue and onomatopoeia, and under-segmented speech balloons. To address these issues, we combine OCR-based issue detection and manual revision to construct Manga109-v2026, revising approximately 29,000 dialogue annotations. Our revisions better align Manga109 with modern OCR and multimodal manga understanding systems while preserving expressive structures characteristic of manga.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Manga109-v2026, an upgraded version of the Manga109 dataset with significant improvements in dialogue annotations to support modern OCR and multimodal manga understanding.
AI.motivation: The current Manga109 dataset contains errors and annotations that are not suitable for modern OCR and multimodal manga understanding tasks, necessitating an updated version.
AI.method: The authors identified five categories of annotation issues in Manga109 and utilized a combination of OCR-based detection and manual revision to correct approximately 29,000 dialogue annotations.
AI.result: Manga109-v2026 was created with revised dialogue annotations, increasing its suitability for advanced OCR and multimodal system applications.
AI.conclusion: The upgrade makes Manga109-v2026 a more accurate and practical dataset for AI-related manga research, preserving manga's expressive structures while enhancing alignment with current technologies.

title: Draw2Think: Harnessing Geometry Reasoning through Constraint Engine Interaction
