The approach includes key features like a queue-style KV caching mechanism for long horizons, anchor-free relational modeling for scalability, and a scale-adaptive geometry loss for metric-scale generalization.
AI.result: UniT achieves state-of-the-art performance across ten benchmarks in seven geometry perception tasks, unifying diverse capabilities within a single framework.
AI.conclusion: This unified model addresses fragmented capabilities, providing a scalable, adaptive solution for dense 3D geometry perception across various tasks and benchmarks.

title: Efficient Banzhaf-Based Data Valuation for $k$-Nearest Neighbors Classification
summary: Data valuation, the task of quantifying the contribution of individual data points to model performance, has emerged as a fundamental challenge in machine learning. Game-theoretic approaches, such as the Banzhaf value, offer principled frameworks for fair data valuation; however, they suffer from exponential computational complexity. We address this challenge by developing efficient algorithms specifically tailored for computing Banzhaf values in $k$-nearest neighbor ($k$NN) classifiers. We first establish the theoretical hardness of the problem by proving that it is \#P-hard. Despite this intractability, we exploit the locality properties of $k$NN classifiers to develop practical exact algorithms. Our main contribution is a dynamic programming framework that achieves significant computational improvements: we present a pseudo-polynomial algorithm with $O(Wkn^2)$ time complexity for weighted $k$NN classifiers, where $W$ is the maximum sum of top-$k$ weights, and a specialized algorithm for unweighted $k$NN that achieves $O(nk^2)$ time complexity, that is, linear in the number of data points. We also offer efficient Monte Carlo estimation methods. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the practical efficiency of our approach and its effectiveness in data valuation applications.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the computational challenges of calculating Banzhaf values in kNN classifiers, proposing efficient algorithms with significant improvements, validated through experiments.
AI.motivation: Understanding the contribution of individual data points to model performance is crucial in machine learning, but existing game-theoretic approaches like the Banzhaf value are computationally expensive.
AI.method: The authors develop exact and efficient algorithms using dynamic programming and Monte Carlo estimation, tailored for weighted and unweighted kNN classifiers.
AI.result: The paper achieves pseudo-polynomial and linear-time algorithms for different kNN types, validated through extensive real-world experiments demonstrating efficiency and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging kNN locality properties, the proposed methods make game-theoretic data valuation feasible, offering scalable and practical solutions.

title: SurgOnAir: Hierarchy-Aware Real-Time Surgical Video Commentary
summary: Understanding surgical workflow in real time is fundamental for intelligent surgical embodiment, where AI systems continuously perceive and respond as surgery proceeds. In the operating room, critical decisions depend on subtle, moment-to-moment changes, such as fine instrument movements and evolving tissue states, where even slight perceptual delays can limit assistance or compromise safety. Yet existing methods remain offline or operate at coarse temporal scales, generating descriptions only after processing clips, preventing immediate reaction. We address this by proposing SurgOnAir, a streaming vision-language model that processes frames sequentially without future access and progressively generates narration tokens as visual input arrives. SurgOnAir achieves fine-grained frame-to-token generation, enabling instant responsiveness to evolving surgical dynamics. Built upon our curated hierarchical dataset SurgOnAir-11k spanning action-, step-, and phase-level supervision, the model is trained to produce multi-level textual responses that reflect the inherent hierarchy of surgical procedures. Furthermore, special transition tokens are generated to explicitly mark state changes, allowing SurgOnAir to capture and signal key workflow transitions as they occur. Experiments show that SurgOnAir enables real-time understanding through a single vision-language model that unifies streaming across multiple hierarchies of the surgical workflow, generating superior and hierarchy-aware narrations. Code and dataset will be public.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SurgOnAir, a vision-language model for real-time surgical workflow understanding that generates narrations and identifies transitions instantaneously as surgery evolves.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of current offline or slow-response surgical assistance systems, enhancing patient safety and intelligent surgical support by enabling real-time, detailed understanding of critical operations.
AI.method: The authors developed SurgOnAir, a streaming vision-language model trained on a curated dataset called SurgOnAir-11k. The model sequentially processes video frames and generates hierarchical textual narrations in real time, incorporating special tokens to mark transitions and state changes.
AI.result: The model demonstrated superior performance in producing timely, hierarchy-aware narrations and successfully captured critical workflow transitions in real time.
AI.conclusion: SurgOnAir unifies real-time surgical workflow understanding across multiple hierarchies, setting the foundation for more responsive and intelligent AI systems in the operating room. The authors will make the code and dataset publicly available for broader use.

title: A Dialogue between Causal and Traditional Representation Learning: Toward Mutual Benefits in a Unified Formulation
summary: Causal representation learning (CRL) and traditional representation learning have largely developed along different trajectories. Traditional representation learning has been driven mainly by applications and empirical objectives, whereas CRL has focused more on theoretical questions, particularly identifiability. This difference in emphasis has created a gap between the two fields in terminology, problem formulation, and evaluation, limiting communication and sometimes leading to disconnected or redundant efforts. In this paper, we argue that these two fields should be brought into dialogue rather than treated as separate paradigms. To this end, we introduce a unified formulation in which the representation learning is characterized by two components: a task component, which specifies what information the learned representation is required to preserve, and a constraint component, which specifies what structure is imposed on the latent space. Under this formulation, the benefits run in both directions. CRL provides theoretical tools for understanding when structured latent constraints are useful or necessary, while traditional representation learning offers practical insights on task design and objective choice that can improve the development of CRL methods. To illustrate this interaction, we experimentally study how different task components affect the behavior of CRL methods under different structured constraints. Results on CausalVerse show that the effectiveness of causal constraints depends strongly on the tasks with which they are paired.

AI.tldr: The paper bridges the gap between causal representation learning (CRL) and traditional representation learning, proposing a unified framework with task and constraint components.
AI.motivation: To reconcile the theoretical focus of CRL with the practical focus of traditional representation learning, acknowledging gaps in terminology, problem formulation, and evaluation.
AI.method: The authors proposed a unified framework to integrate task components (preserving information in learned representations) with constraint components (defining latent space structure).
AI.result: Experimental results show that the effectiveness of causal constraints strongly depends on the pairing with specific tasks, demonstrated on CausalVerse.
AI.conclusion: Combining theoretical insights from CRL and practical objectives from traditional representation learning enriches both fields and improves task-specific outcomes.

title: Distill to Think, Foresee to Act: Cognitive-Physical Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Driving
summary: Current end-to-end autonomous driving models are fundamentally constrained by the behavioral cloning ceiling of imitation learning. While reinforcement learning offers a path to smarter autonomy, it demands two missing pieces of infrastructure: (1) a cognitive foundation that understands traffic semantics and driving intent, and (2) a foresighted physical environment that can anticipate the consequences of candidate actions. To this end, we propose CoPhy, a CognitivePhysical reinforcement learning framework for autonomous driving. To distill to think, we distill VLM knowledge into the BEV encoder and then discard the VLM entirely, retaining cognitive ability at zero inference cost while releasing the cognitive channel as a pluggable interface for optional human language commands. To foresee to act, we build an auto-regressive BEV world model that explicitly predicts future semantic maps conditioned on candidate actions, serving as an interpretable physical sandbox from which safety metrics are directly derived. Built upon this dual infrastructure, we optimize the driving policy via GRPO with a novel dual-reward mechanism: a physical reward derived from BEV rollouts enforces hard safety constraints, while a cognitive reward from a language-aligned scorer ensures intent compliance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CoPhy not only achieves state-of-the-art results on NAVSIM v1 and v2 benchmarks, but also enables safer driving via cognitively informed scene compliance and flexible intent control through user-defined language instructions.

AI.tldr: CoPhy proposes an innovative reinforcement learning framework for autonomous driving by integrating cognitive and physical simulation components, achieving improved safety, intent alignment, and adaptability.
AI.motivation: Current end-to-end driving models are limited by imitation learning's constraints, and improving autonomous driving necessitates integrating understanding of traffic semantics and predicting action consequences.
AI.method: The CoPhy framework incorporates cognitive understanding through visual-language model (VLM) knowledge distilled into BEV encoders and introduces an auto-regressive BEV world model for predicting semantic map futures, along with a dual-reward system for policy optimization.
AI.result: CoPhy achieved state-of-the-art performance on NAVSIM v1 and v2 benchmarks and demonstrated enhanced safety, scene compliance, and flexibility in responding to user-defined language instructions.
AI.conclusion: The research showcases the viability of combining cognitive and predictive physical simulations for smarter, safer, and more flexible autonomous driving, leveraging reinforcement learning principles.

title: Robust Personalized Recommendation under Hidden Confounding in MNAR
summary: Recommender systems often rely on observational user--item interaction data, which is prone to selection bias due to users' selective interactions with items. Inverse propensity weighting and doubly robust estimators effectively mitigate selection bias under observed confounding, but are unreliable in the presence of hidden confounders. Existing approaches relying on randomized controlled trials (RCTs) or global sensitivity bounds are constrained in practice: RCTs demand costly experimental data, while global sensitivity bounds presume a uniformly bounded effect of unmeasured confounders on propensities through sensitivity analysis, thereby neglecting heterogeneity across user--item interactions. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel framework, which estimates user--item level sensitivity bounds, thereby substantially relaxing the homogeneity assumption inherent in global sensitivity bounds named Personalized Unobserved-Confounding-aware Interaction Deconfounder (PUID). To ensure both robustness and predictive accuracy, we further develop an adversarial optimization strategy and propose a benchmark-guided variant (BPUID) that incorporates pre-trained models as stabilizing references. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms global methods under hidden confounding, without requiring RCT data.

AI.tldr: The paper presents PUID and BPUID frameworks to address selection bias in recommender systems caused by hidden confounders, outperforming existing sensitivity methods without relying on costly RCTs.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of traditional sensitivity bounds in recommender systems that inadequately handle hidden confounding due to reliance on uniform assumptions or costly RCTs.
AI.method: Propose Personalized Unobserved-Confounding-aware Interaction Deconfounder (PUID) for user-item level sensitivity bounds estimation, along with a benchmark-guided variant (BPUID) leveraging adversarial optimization and pre-trained models.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets show that PUID and BPUID significantly outperform existing global sensitivity methods under hidden confounding conditions.
AI.conclusion: PUID and BPUID provide effective, practical solutions for mitigating selection bias in recommendation systems without requiring RCTs, by addressing the heterogeneity of user-item interactions.

title: FTerViT: Fully Ternary Vision Transformer
summary: Ternary Vision Transformers offer substantial model compression, however state-of-the-art methods only ternarize the encoder layers, leaving patch embeddings, LayerNorm parameters, and classifier heads in full precision. In compact models targeting resource-constrained processors, such as microcontrollers, these remaining full-precision components determine the total memory footprint, severely limiting deployment efficiency and on-device feasibility. In this work, we introduce a fully ternarized Vision Transformer in which \emph{all} weight matrices and normalization parameters are ternarized (FTerViT). To this end, we introduce two novel operators : TernaryBitConv2d with per-channel scaling for patch embedding and TernaryLayerNorm. FTerViT is trained using knowledge distillation, followed by a lightweight quantization-aware recovery phase. Our ternary W2A8 DeiT-III-S at 384$\times$384 resolution achieves 82.43\% ImageNet-1K top-1 at 6.09\,MB (${\sim}$15$\times$ compression, $-$2.42\,pp vs.\ FP32), outperforming prior ternary ViTs methods up to 8 pp. Finally, we demonstrate the first implementation of ternary vision transformers on a dual cores XTensa LX7 microcontroller inside the ESP32-S3 system-on-chip. By deploying FTerViT-Small (based on DeiT-III-Small at 224$\times$224 resolution, 5.81\,MB), we achieve 79.64\% ImageNet-1K top-1 accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes FTerViT, a fully ternarized Vision Transformer where all components are ternarized to improve memory and deployment efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing ternary Vision Transformers leave key components in full precision, restricting memory usage and feasibility on resource-constrained devices.
AI.method: FTerViT ternarizes all weight matrices and normalization parameters, introduces novel operators (TernaryBitConv2d, TernaryLayerNorm), and employs knowledge distillation with quantization-aware recovery.
AI.result: FTerViT achieves 82.43% ImageNet-1K top-1 accuracy with significant compression and demonstrates feasibility on microcontrollers (ESP32-S3).
AI.conclusion: The proposed fully ternarized Vision Transformer supports deployment on constrained hardware, outperforming previous ternary methods in accuracy and compression.

title: Towards Understanding Self-Pretraining for Sequence Classification
comment: v1: Preliminary, extension of the version accepted at ICML 2025 Workshop MOSS
summary: Amos et al. (2024) showed that the accuracy of Transformer models in sequence classification can be significantly improved by first pretraining with a masked token prediction objective without external data or augmentation, a procedure referred to as self-pretraining (SPT). While the primary objective of Amos et al. (2024) was to showcase that Transformers can achieve strong performance on the Long-Range Arena (LRA), their pipeline raises more fundamental questions: How does SPT drive optimization to better solutions? Why can standard supervised training fail in Transformers? To better understand this, we replicate and systematically ablate the findings of Amos et al. (2024). Our ablations suggest that a central bottleneck in the studied settings is not depth or generalization alone, but the ability of label supervision to learn useful query-key Attention patterns from random initialization. With a minimal setup, we identify learning proximity interactions - turning absolute positional encodings into proximity-biased Attention scores - as a key source of the improvements brought by SPT. Finally, in a simplified theoretical setup, we show that label supervision can be locally blind to certain Attention-score directions that are instead detectable through masked reconstruction.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how self-pretraining (SPT) improves Transformer performance in sequence classification, replicating and extending prior findings to identify key mechanisms behind the benefits of SPT.
AI.motivation: To better understand why SPT helps Transformers surpass standard supervised training and optimize Attention mechanisms, addressing fundamental model challenges highlighted by previous research.
AI.method: The authors replicated and systematically ablated results from Amos et al. (2024), identifying the role of Attention patterns and proximity-biased scores through minimal setups and simplified theoretical analyses.
AI.result: They found label supervision struggles with learning effective Attention patterns from scratch, and demonstrated how SPT promotes proximity-focused interactions that standard training fails to capture.
AI.conclusion: SPT enhances Transformer performance by overcoming limitations in label-supervised training, particularly improving the model's ability to learn meaningful Attention patterns and positional encodings.

title: SAM-Sode: Towards Faithful Explanations for Tiny Bacteria Detection
summary: Interpretability in object detection provides crucial confidence support for clinical auxiliary diagnosis. However, in tiny bacteria detection, traditional explanation methods often suffer from blurred foreground boundaries and diffuse feature attribution due to the extreme sparsity of target morphological features and severe interference from complex backgrounds. Such limitations hinder the provision of logically coherent morphological evidence. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel eXplainable AI (XAI) framework, SAM-Sode. The framework innovatively transforms initial feature attribution maps into geometry-aware prompts, leveraging the prior knowledge of the foundation model (SAM3) to achieve spatial refinement and morphological reconstruction of the explanatory mappings. Furthermore, we introduce a dual-constraint mechanism based on physical significance and geometric alignment to perform instance-level denoising, generating coherent explanations that better align with human expert intuition. Experimental results on our self-constructed bacteria dataset with complex circuit backgrounds (containing 2,524 images) and other public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method effectively suppresses background redundancy and significantly enhances the decision-making transparency of tiny object detection.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SAM-Sode, an explainable AI framework to improve interpretability in tiny bacteria detection by refining feature attribution maps and reducing background noise.
AI.motivation: Traditional explanation methods fail in tiny bacteria detection due to blurred boundaries and complex background interference, making it hard to provide logical evidence for clinical diagnosis.
AI.method: The authors propose SAM-Sode, which converts feature attribution maps into geometry-aware prompts, uses a foundation model (SAM3) for spatial refinement, and applies dual constraints for instance-level denoising.
AI.result: The method suppresses background noise and improves decision transparency in bacterial object detection, as validated by tests on a custom dataset with 2,524 images and public datasets.
AI.conclusion: SAM-Sode enhances interpretability and reliability in tiny bacteria detection, aligning explanations more closely with expert intuition by refining spatial and morphological details.

title: Reviving Error Correction in Modern Deep Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Modern deep-learning models have achieved remarkable success in time-series forecasting. Yet, their performance degrades in long-term prediction due to error accumulation in autoregressive inference, where predictions are recursively used as inputs. While classical error correction mechanisms (ECMs) have long been used in statistical methods, their applicability to deep learning models remains limited or ineffective. In this work, we revisit the error accumulation problem in deep time-series forecasting and investigate the role and necessity of ECMs in this new context. We propose a simple, architecture-agnostic error correction model that can be integrated with any existing forecaster without requiring retraining. By explicitly decomposing predictions into trend and seasonal components and training the corrector to adjust each separately, we introduce the Universal Error Corrector with Seasonal-Trend Decomposition (UEC-STD), which significantly improves correction accuracy and robustness across 4 backbones and 10 datasets. Our findings provide a practical tool for enhancing forecasts while offering new insights into mitigating autoregressive errors in deep time-series models. Code is available at https://github.com/DA2I2-SLM/UEC-STD.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Universal Error Corrector with Seasonal-Trend Decomposition (UEC-STD) that addresses error accumulation in deep-learning-based time-series forecasting, significantly enhancing prediction accuracy and robustness.
AI.motivation: The motivation lies in overcoming the challenge of error accumulation during long-term autoregressive inference in deep time-series forecasting models, as conventional error correction methods are either incompatible or ineffective in this context.
AI.method: The authors propose UEC-STD, a model-agnostic error correction approach that decomposes predictions into trend and seasonal components, training a corrector to adjust each component separately. This method works without retraining the base forecasting model.
AI.result: UEC-STD significantly improves correction accuracy and robustness across 4 different backbone models and 10 datasets.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a practical, architecture-agnostic solution to enhance deep-learning time-series forecasts while advancing understanding of autoregressive error correction mechanisms.

title: Lower Bounds for Advection-Diffusion Equations: An Exploration with AI-Generated Proofs
summary:
We establish explicit lower bounds for advection-diffusion equations in three settings: a polynomial $\dot H^{-1}$ bound for inviscid shears with $u\in L^\infty_t W^{1,1}_y$, a uniform positive lower bound on the mixing scale for diffusive shears, and an exponential $L^2$ bound for rapidly oscillating time-periodic flows. All constants are explicit in the data.
  The proofs were generated entirely by a multi-agent math proving system, QED, without expert human intervention, serving as a test of AI's capability to produce rigorous mathematics.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes explicit lower bounds for different advection-diffusion equation settings using a multi-agent math proving system, QED.
AI.motivation: To test the capability of AI, specifically a multi-agent proving system called QED, in producing rigorous mathematical results.
AI.method: Developed proofs for lower bounds in advection-diffusion equations for various conditions using QED, an AI-driven proving system, with no human expert intervention.
AI.result: Established three distinct lower bounds: a polynomial $\dot H^{-1}$ bound for inviscid shears, a uniform mixing scale lower bound for diffusive shears, and an exponential $L^2$ bound for oscillating time-periodic flows. All constants were explicit.
AI.conclusion: This paper demonstrates that AI systems like QED can autonomously establish rigorous mathematical proofs and contribute to advancements in mathematical theory.

title: Semantic Granularity Navigation in Image Editing
summary: Despite the generative capabilities of diffusion and flow models, real-image editing remains constrained by a persistent trade-off between semantic editability and structural fidelity. We trace a primary cause of this limitation to the implicit coupling of edit progress with model scale in existing paradigms. Under this coupling, stronger edits typically require visiting noisier states, which spends computation on destabilizing layout before the semantic change is well localized. We introduce NaviEdit, a training-free inference-time controller that decouples edit progress from model scale traversal through a strict self-consistency contract. NaviEdit operates at the rollout level and leaves the underlying pretrained model unchanged. It treats scale as a control input and reallocates a fixed step budget toward semantically responsive intermediate scales instead of destructive high-noise regimes. Experiments show positive average gains across compatible editors and flow backbones, supporting decoupling as a portable inference-time control principle.

AI.tldr: The study introduces NaviEdit, a training-free method to improve real-image editing by addressing the trade-off between semantic editability and structural fidelity in diffusion and flow models.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to tackle the inherent trade-off in real-image editing, where achieving stronger semantic edits compromises structural fidelity due to existing models’ coupling of edit progress with model scale.
AI.method: The authors propose NaviEdit, an inference-time controller that decouples edit progress from model scale traversal using self-consistency contracts. NaviEdit reassigns computational effort towards semantically relevant scales rather than noisy, destructive states.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that NaviEdit achieves improved performance across different editors and flow backbones, supporting its effectiveness in maintaining both editability and fidelity.
AI.conclusion: NaviEdit is a promising, portable technique for real-image editing, offering a new inference-time control principle to separate semantic changes from structural destabilization.

title: UOTIP: Unbalanced Optimal Transport Map for Unpaired Inverse Problems
summary: We investigate unpaired image inverse problems, a challenging setting where only independent, non-paired sets of noisy measurements and clean target signals are available for training. We propose a novel inverse problem solver based on Unbalanced Optimal Transport, called Unbalanced Optimal Transport Map for Inverse Problems (UOTIP). Our method formulates the reconstruction task, predicting clean target signals from noisy measurements, as learning a UOT Map from noisy measurement distribution to clean signal distribution by incorporating a likelihood-based cost function. By relaxing the exact marginal constraint, the UOT framework provides key advantages to our model: robustness to multi-level observation noise, adaptability to class imbalance between noisy and clean datasets, and generalizability to diverse noise-type scenarios. Furthermore, we theoretically demonstrate that incorporating a quadratic cost term ensures the existence and uniqueness of the transport map by satisfying the twist condition, even for ill-posed inverse problems. Our experiments demonstrate that UOTIP achieves state-of-the-art performance on unpaired image inverse problem benchmarks, across linear and nonlinear inverse problems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces UOTIP, a method for solving unpaired image inverse problems using Unbalanced Optimal Transport (UOT), showing state-of-the-art results on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of unpaired image inverse problems, where only unpaired noisy measurements and clean signals are available, by providing a robust and generalizable solution.
AI.method: The authors formulate a UOT-based reconstruction method, incorporating a likelihood cost function and relaxing marginal constraints, making it robust and adaptable to noise variability and dataset imbalance.
AI.result: UOTIP demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks for both linear and nonlinear unpaired image inverse problems.
AI.conclusion: UOTIP proves to be a theoretically sound and practically effective solution, providing robustness, adaptability, and uniqueness for solving unpaired image inverse problems.

title: RankE: End-to-End Post-Training for Discrete Text-to-Image Generation with Decoder Co-Evolution
summary: Discrete autoregressive (AR) text-to-image (T2I) models pair a VQ tokenizer with an AR policy, and current post-training pipelines optimize only the policy while keeping the VQ decoder frozen. Recent diffusion T2I work, exemplified by REPA-E, has shown that the VAE itself constitutes a key alignment bottleneck, yet no analogous investigation exists for discrete AR models. We show that policy-only optimization induces Latent Covariate Shift: as the policy evolves, the resulting token distribution diverges from the ground-truth distribution on which the decoder was trained, such that reward scores improve while decoded image quality degrades. To address this mismatch, we propose RankE, the first end-to-end post-training framework for discrete T2I generation. Rather than optimizing the policy against a fixed decoder, RankE co-evolves both components through alternating optimization: each module maximizes a ranking-based alignment objective while being regularized by a stability-preserving anchor suited to its parameter space. This co-evolution breaks the fidelity--alignment trade-off that plagues frozen-decoder approaches: on LlamaGen-XL (775M), standard RL improves CLIP but degrades FID, whereas RankE improves both simultaneously (FID 15.21, CLIP 33.76 on MS-COCO 30K). Consistent gains on Janus-Pro (1B) confirm that decoder co-evolution reliably converts reward optimization into pixel-space quality improvements.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes RankE, a method to address latent covariate shift in discrete AR text-to-image models by co-optimizing both the AR policy and decoder, improving both alignment and image quality.
AI.motivation: Addressing the latent covariate shift caused by policy-only optimization in discrete autoregressive T2I models, which lowers decoded image quality despite improved reward scores.
AI.method: RankE implements alternating optimization where both the policy and decoder evolve together using a ranking-based alignment objective with stability-preserving regularization.
AI.result: The proposed RankE method improves both FID and CLIP scores simultaneously, outperforming standard RL approaches in terms of image quality and alignment.
AI.conclusion: Co-evolving the AR policy and decoder is essential for breaking the trade-off between fidelity and alignment in discrete T2I frameworks, as demonstrated by improvements on both LlamaGen-XL and Janus-Pro models.

title: A Typed Tensor Language for Federated Learning
summary: Federated learning and analytics are often described as collections of separate protocols, even when they share the same mathematical form: client-local tensor computation, mergeable aggregation into shared state, and shared-only post-processing. We introduce a typed tensor language that formalizes this structure. The language distinguishes federated tensors, whose records are partitioned across clients along a tracked record axis, from shared tensors, which are available globally. Its semantics are defined by comparison with a virtual global tensor, used only as a reference object. The main result is a shared-state factorization theory. We show that typed one-round programs factor through fixed-dimensional shared state whose size is independent of the number of clients and records, computed from client-local tensor expressions and merged across clients. We also prove a converse representability result; factorizations whose encoders and decoders are expressible in the language are realized by typed one-round programs, and the correspondence extends to iterative programs whose cross-round state is shared. This gives a formal account of the computations in the language that can be expressed as encode, merge, and decode procedures. We then develop a differentiable fragment for learning. If a per-record loss and its per-record gradient are represented by client-local tensor expressions, the global gradient is represented by record-axis summation of the federated gradient tensor. This yields typed iterative programs for server-side gradient descent and shared-linear-algebra second-order updates. The framework characterizes a broad class of federated learning computations whose communication passes through fixed-dimensional shared state.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a formalized approach to federated learning using typed tensor language to structure computations between federated and shared tensors, ensuring efficient communication and server-side updates.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to unify and extend the theoretical understanding of federated learning computations, which often exist as separate protocols despite sharing common mathematical structures.
AI.method: They propose a typed tensor language distinguishing federated tensors from shared tensors, using a shared-state factorization theory and semantic comparisons with virtual global tensors.
AI.result: The authors provide theoretical results that show one-round programs can factor through fixed-dimensional shared states, introduce representability proofs, and extend this framework to iterative programs and differentiable learning tasks.
AI.conclusion: This framework offers a formal characterization of federated learning computations, enabling efficient encode-merge-decode processes and server-side optimization techniques for scalable learning across clients.

title: PGC: Peak-Guided Calibration for Generalizable AI-Generated Image Detection
summary: The rapid evolution of generative AI, from GANs to modern diffusion models, has resulted in increasingly subtle discriminative clues. These fine-grained signals are often overshadowed by dominant, high-fidelity image content (e.g., the main subject), limiting the reliability of existing detectors that predominantly rely on global representations. To address this challenge, we propose the Peak-Guided Calibration (PGC) framework. PGC introduces a novel strategy that aggregates salient features via a peak-focusing mechanism. Specifically, by employing a peak-sensitive aggregation that accentuates the most discriminative local clues, PGC leverages these critical signals to calibrate the global decision. This approach recovers subtle patterns that would otherwise be submerged in the global context. Furthermore, to better simulate real-world threats, we introduce the CommGen15 dataset, a challenging benchmark comprising samples from 15 commercial models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PGC achieves state-of-the-art performance. Specifically, it improves mean accuracy by +12.3% on our CommGen15 dataset, and sets new records on standard benchmarks, including GenImage (+2.1%), AIGI (+3.5%), and UniversalFakeDetect (+1.7%). Code is available at https://github.com/xiaoyu6868/PGC.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Peak-Guided Calibration (PGC) framework for detecting generative AI content and achieves superior performance on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: With generative AI models producing increasingly subtle and high-quality outputs, existing detectors struggle to reliably identify generated content due to reliance on global representations overshadowed by dominant image content.
AI.method: The proposed PGC framework employs a peak-focusing mechanism to highlight and aggregate salient local discriminative features, allowing the recovery of critical subtle patterns for improved global decision-making.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance of PGC, including a +12.3% mean accuracy improvement on the new CommGen15 dataset and record-breaking results on GenImage (+2.1%), AIGI (+3.5%), and UniversalFakeDetect (+1.7%).
AI.conclusion: The PGC framework effectively enhances the detection of generative AI content by focusing on subtle local features, setting new benchmarks in accuracy, and providing a promising tool for addressing real-world detection challenges.

title: HORST: Composing Optimizer Geometries for Sparse Transformer Training
summary: Sparsifying transformers remains a fundamental challenge, as standard optimizers fail to simultaneously encourage sparsity and maintain training stability. Effective adaptive optimizers exhibit an implicit $L_{\infty}$ bias favoring stability, yet, sparsity requires an $L_1$ bias. To integrate sparsity, we propose a composition of optimizer steps, which we cast as non-commutative operators to analyze and combine their optimization geometry in a principled way. This yields HORST (Hyperbolic Operator for Robust Sparse Training), a modular optimizer that inherits stability from adaptive methods while inducing $L_1$ sparsity bias through a hyperbolic mirror map. Our experiments demonstrate its utility for sparse training of transformers on both vision and language tasks. HORST consistently and significantly outperforms AdamW baselines across all sparsity levels, with large gains at higher sparsity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new optimizer, HORST, for sparse training in transformers, addressing the balance between stability and sparsity.
AI.motivation: Standard optimizers struggle to effectively integrate sparsity and maintain stability during transformer training.
AI.method: The authors devise a modular optimizer, HORST, using a hyperbolic mirror map to combine stability-enhancing adaptive methods with $L_1$ sparsity bias.
AI.result: HORST outperforms the AdamW optimizer across varying sparsity levels, showing significant improvements at higher sparsity thresholds.
AI.conclusion: HORST effectively addresses the challenge of sparsifying transformers by ensuring stability while promoting sparsity, proving its utility in vision and language tasks.

title: RePCM: Region-Specific and Phenotype-Adaptive Bi-Ventricular Cardiac Motion Synthesis
comment: Early Accepted by MICCAI 2026. This is the author's submitted version. 10 pages, 3 figures
summary: Cardiac motion over a cardiac cycle is crucial for quantifying regional function and is strongly affected by cardiovascular diseases. Since temporally dense mesh sequences are difficult to obtain in practice, we focus on leveraging the more accessible end-diastolic frame to infer a full-cycle sequence. Due to strong regional and disease-specific differences, traditional methods often oversmooth the data by relying on generative models that are optimized for global patterns. To address this problem, we propose Region-Aware and Phenotype-Adaptive Bi-Ventricular Cardiac Motion Synthesis (RePCM) for single frame Bi-ventricular mesh motion completion. In Stage I, a reconstruction network learns vertex wise motion descriptors and clustering yields a data driven functional partition, providing an explicit motion derived region structure. In Stage II, a Region-Specific Injection Module enforces masked, synchronized region exchange within a conditional VAE, preserving localized specific dynamics and restricting cross-region mixing. A Phenotype-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts prior conditioned on ED shape uses anatomy-guided cues to model latent motion trends and capture inter-disease variability. Experiments on three datasets covering different cardiovascular diseases show consistent gains in geometric and functional metrics and improved preservation of region specific dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper focuses on synthesizing temporally dense cardiac motion data using end-diastolic frames, and proposes a novel Region-Aware and Phenotype-Adaptive model to address challenges related to regional and disease-specific differences.
AI.motivation: Obtaining temporally dense mesh sequences to quantify cardiac motion is difficult in practice, and traditional models fail to accurately capture regional and disease-specific dynamics.
AI.method: The proposed method involves a two-stage approach: a reconstruction network for deriving regional partitioning based on motion descriptors, followed by a Region-Specific Injection Module and phenotype-adaptive mixture models to preserve dynamics and inter-disease variability.
AI.result: The model showed consistent improvements in geometric and functional metrics across diverse datasets, with enhanced preservation of localized and regional dynamics.
AI.conclusion: RePCM effectively synthesizes full-cycle cardiac motion using limited input data, demonstrating utility in studying regional cardiac dynamics while addressing disease-specific variability.

title: Trusted Weights, Treacherous Optimizations? Optimization-Triggered Backdoor Attacks on LLMs
summary: Inference optimization is a vital technique for deploying LLMs at scale. Compilation is the most widely adopted optimization technique for LLMs. While it assumes semantic equivalence between the original and compiled graphs, we first uncover its numerical side effects can be maliciously exploited to implant stealthy backdoors in LLMs. We propose a unified optimization-triggered attack framework comprising two complementary strategies. Without any modification to the compiler or hardware, one strategy flips predictions for specific inputs only when the model is compiled, while the other uses a universal trigger that remains dormant under uncompiled execution but hijacks arbitrary inputs once compilation optimization is applied. Both attacks bypass standard safety evaluations run without compilation. We empirically demonstrate that these optimization-triggered backdoors achieve attack success rates averaging 90% across four mainstream open-source LLMs and four tasks, while clean accuracy is preserved at nearly 100% under all settings. Our findings reveal a novel attack surface at the intersection of optimization and security in the LLM deployment pipeline, and we investigate practical defenses to mitigate this threat.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals how compilation-based optimizations for LLMs can be exploited to implant stealthy backdoors, achieving high attack success rates without degrading clean accuracy.
AI.motivation: To explore the potential security risks of LLM compilation-based inference optimization, which is widely used for scaling LLM performance.
AI.method: Propose a unified optimization-triggered attack framework with two strategies: 1) Specific input-triggered attacks appearing only post-compilation, and 2) Universal triggers dormant in uncompiled models but active post-compilation.
AI.result: Achieved an average attack success rate of 90% across four open-source LLMs and four tasks while maintaining nearly 100% clean accuracy.
AI.conclusion: LLM deployment pipelines using compilation open a novel attack surface; practical defenses are necessary to mitigate this threat.

title: SR-Ground: Image Quality Grounding for Super-Resolved Content
summary:
Super-Resolution (SR) has advanced rapidly in recent years, with diffusion-based models achieving unprecedented fidelity at the cost of introducing new types of visual artifacts. While existing Image Quality Assessment (IQA) methods provide holistic quality scores, they lack interpretability and fail to distinguish between different artifact types arising from modern SR approaches.
  To address this gap, we introduce SR-Ground, a large-scale dataset specifically designed for fine-grained artifact segmentation in super-resolved images. The dataset comprises images processed by a diverse set of state-of-the-art SR models, with pixel-level annotations for multiple artifact categories. We conduct a large-scale crowdsourcing study involving 1,062 participants to validate and refine automatically generated segmentations, resulting in a high-quality dataset of 63,000 images spanning 6 distinct artifact types.
  We demonstrate that training IQA models with grounding capabilities on SR-Ground significantly improves performance on downstream tasks. Furthermore, we introduce a fine-tuning pipeline that leverages our grounding model to reduce perceptible artifacts in SR outputs, showcasing the practical utility of our dataset.

AI.tldr: The paper presents SR-Ground, a dataset for fine-grained artifact segmentation in super-resolved images, along with methods to improve IQA models and reduce perceptible artifacts in SR outputs.
AI.motivation: Existing Image Quality Assessment (IQA) methods lack interpretability and cannot distinguish between different artifact types from modern Super-Resolution (SR) approaches.
AI.method: The authors created SR-Ground, a dataset of 63,000 images with pixel-level annotations for six artifact types, validated via a crowdsourcing study of 1,062 participants. They also propose using this dataset to train IQA models for artifact-specific analysis and a fine-tuning pipeline for artifact reduction.
AI.result: Training IQA models on SR-Ground enhances performance on downstream quality assessment tasks. The fine-tuning pipeline demonstrates practical effectiveness in reducing artifacts in SR outputs.
AI.conclusion: SR-Ground facilitates interpretable IQA and artifact mitigation, addressing limitations of current methods and showcasing its practical benefits for advanced SR applications.

title: Efficient Learning of Deep State Space Models via Importance Smoothing
comment: Accepted to the proceedings of ICML 2026
summary: Latent state space systems are ubiquitous in statistical modelling, arising naturally when a time series is observed through a noisy measurement function, however training deep state space models (DSSM) at scale remains difficult. Two largely distinct strategies and literatures have developed around the training of DSSMs. Firstly, auto-encoding DSSMs train generative DSSMs by optimising a variational lower bound. Secondly, DSSMs trained by back-propagating the outputs of a classical sequential Monte Carlo algorithm (SMC). Such approaches can train DSSMs for discriminative as well as generative tasks, however, due to the sequentiality of their forward pass, scale poorly on modern hardware. We propose a new training method \emph{parallel variational Monte Carlo} (PVMC) that bridges the gap between the paradigms, and can be used robustly to train DSSMs for both discriminative and generative tasks. Our method achieves state-of-the-art or better results on a set of baseline experiments and trains $10\times$ faster than the fastest competing SMC approach.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Parallel Variational Monte Carlo (PVMC), a training method that enhances the scalability and efficiency of deep state space models (DSSMs) for generative and discriminative tasks.
AI.motivation: Training DSSMs at scale remains challenging due to limitations in existing methods, like the sequential and hardware-incompatible nature of sequential Monte Carlo-based approaches.
AI.method: The paper introduces PVMC, a method that fuses ideas from auto-encoding and Monte Carlo approaches to train DSSMs in a highly parallel and efficient manner.
AI.result: PVMC achieved state-of-the-art or superior results in benchmark tasks and trains 10× faster compared to the fastest sequential Monte Carlo methods.
AI.conclusion: PVMC effectively bridges the gap between existing methods for DSSM training and provides significant improvements in both scalability and performance.

title: STiTch: Semantic Transition and Transportation in Collaboration for Training-Free Zero-Shot Composed Image Retrieval
summary: Training-free zero-shot composed image retrieval models are recently gaining increasing research interest due to their generalizability and flexibility in unseen multimodal retrieval. Recent LLM-based advances focus on generating the expected target caption by exploring the compositional ability behind the LLMs. Although efficient, we find that 1) the generated captions tend to introduce unexpected features from the reference image due to the semantic gap between the input image and text modification, where the image contains much more details than the text; 2) the point-to-point alignment during the retrieval stage fails to capture diverse compositions. To address these challenges, we introduce a novel Semantic Transition and Transportation in collaboration framework for training-free zero-shot CIR tasks. Specifically, given the composed caption inferred by an LLM, we aim to refine it through a transition vector in the embedding space and make it closer to the target image. Combining LLMs with user instruction, the refined caption concentrates more on the core modification intent and thus filters out unnecessary noise. Moreover, to explore diverse alignment during the retrieval stage, we model the caption and image as discrete distributions and reformulate the retrieval task as a set-to-set alignment task. Finally, a bidirectional transportation distance is developed to consider fine-grained alignments across modalities and calculate the retrieval score. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method can be general, effective, and beneficial for many CIR tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel framework for training-free zero-shot composed image retrieval (CIR), focusing on semantic refinement of captions and improved alignment strategies.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of existing zero-shot CIR methods, such as unexpected features in generated captions and the inability to capture diverse compositions in retrieval.
AI.method: The proposed method refines captions generated by LLMs using a transition vector and employs a bidirectional transportation distance for set-to-set alignment during retrieval.
AI.result: Extensive experiments reveal that the proposed approach is general and effective across various CIR tasks, improving retrieval performance.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively tackles challenges in zero-shot CIR by refining captions and introducing better alignment techniques, benefiting multimodal retrieval applications.

title: A Unified Framework for Uncertainty-Aware Explainable Artificial Intelligence: A Case Study in Power Quality Disturbance Classification
summary: Post-hoc explainable AI (XAI) methods typically produce deterministic attribution maps, whereas Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) induce a distribution over explanations. Capturing the variability of this distribution is important for uncertainty-aware decision-making. This paper formalises the \emph{explanation distribution} as the push-forward measure of the BNN posterior through any Lipschitz-continuous attribution operator. It further proposes the uncertainty-aware relevance attribution operator (UA-RAO), a general family of operators that summarises the explanation distribution using the mean, variance, coefficient of variation, quantiles, and set-theoretic aggregation measures. Theoretical support is provided through Monte Carlo accessibility and Wasserstein approximation bounds. The framework is evaluated on a 15-class power quality disturbance (PQD) classification benchmark, comparing three BNN approximations paired with three attribution operators using relevance mass accuracy and intersection-over-union as localisation metrics. Results show that deep ensembles with the mean UA-RAO improve localisation over the deterministic baseline, while other UA-RAO summaries reveal uncertainty patterns absent from point-estimate attributions. Qualitative results on measured signals further suggest that these patterns generalise beyond the synthetic training distribution. The framework is domain-agnostic and can be applied to any BNN paired with a Lipschitz-continuous attribution operator.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework for uncertainty-aware explainable AI (XAI) by characterizing explanation distributions in Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) and providing theoretical and empirical evaluations.
AI.motivation: Standard XAI methods only offer deterministic attribution maps, ignoring the variability in explanations that can help in uncertainty-aware decision-making. Bayesian neural networks naturally represent such uncertainty, necessitating a formal framework to interpret it.
AI.method: The paper formalizes the explanation distribution as a push-forward measure of the BNN posterior and introduces UA-RAO, a family of operators summarizing explanation distributions using statistical metrics like mean, variance, and quantiles. Theoretical Monte Carlo accessibility and Wasserstein bounds support the approach, and evaluation is performed on a power quality disturbance classification task.
AI.result: The framework shows that deep ensembles combined with the mean UA-RAO improve localisation accuracy compared to deterministic baselines, while other summaries expose uncertainty patterns. Results generalize well beyond training data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is effective, domain-agnostic, and provides improved and more informative uncertainty-aware attributions, making it suitable for various BNN applications.

title: Vision Transformers and Convolutional Neural Networks for Land Use Scene Classification
summary: Land Use Scene Classification (LUSC) from remote sensing imagery plays a critical role in environmental monitoring, urban planning, and sustainable resource management. In recent years, deep learning methods have significantly advanced the state of the art, with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) dominating the field because of their strong ability to capture local spatial features. However, the emergence of Vision Transformers (ViTs) has introduced a new paradigm that models long-range dependencies through self-attention mechanisms, potentially enabling improved global context understanding. This paper presents a comparative assessment of Vision Transformers and CNN-based architecture for remote sensing land use scene classification. Representative CNN models, such as AlexNet, is evaluated alongside the Vision Transformer (ViT) using benchmark remote sensing datasets, including the UC Merced Land Use and EuroSAT Land Use datasets. The study examines classification accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and computational complexity to provide a comprehensive performance comparison. Experimental results demonstrate that CNNs perform robustly on datasets with limited training samples and strong local texture characteristics, whereas Vision Transformers exhibit superior performance in capturing global spatial relationships in complex scenes when sufficient training data are available. However, ViTs typically require greater computational resources and larger training datasets to achieve optimal performance. The findings of this study provide insights into the strengths and limitations of both architectures and offer guidance for selecting appropriate models for remote sensing land use scene classification applications.

AI.tldr: This paper compares Vision Transformers (ViTs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for remote sensing land use scene classification, focusing on their performance and computational demands.
AI.motivation: To evaluate and contrast the performance of CNNs and ViTs in land use scene classification, given the increasing importance of understanding local and global spatial relationships in remote sensing imagery.
AI.method: Representative CNN models (e.g., AlexNet) and Vision Transformers are tested using benchmark datasets (UC Merced Land Use and EuroSAT). Metrics such as classification accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and computational complexity are analyzed.
AI.result: CNNs excel in scenarios with limited data and local texture-laden images, while ViTs perform better for global spatial relationships in complex scenes provided abundant training data and computational resources.
AI.conclusion: The study emphasizes the need for model selection based on dataset characteristics, computational requirements, and spatial relationship needs for effective remote sensing land use classification.

title: Advantage Collapse in Group Relative Policy Optimization: Diagnosis and Mitigation
comment: 26 pages, 12 figures. Accepted at the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a prominent algorithm within the Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) framework, has achieved strong results in improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). However, GRPO is prone to advantage collapse, a failure mode where homogeneous rewards within a group (e.g., all correct or all incorrect answers) yield near-zero advantages and vanishing gradients. To address this, we introduce the Advantage Collapse Rate (ACR), the first diagnostic metric quantifying the proportion of training batches with ineffective gradients. Across models from 0.5B to 14B parameters on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, we show that ACR strongly predicts training stagnation and final performance. We then propose Adaptive Virtual Sample Policy Optimization (AVSPO), a lightweight extension of GRPO that injects virtual reward samples, guided by real-time ACR monitoring, to enable learning from homogeneous groups without additional model rollouts. AVSPO reduces advantage collapse by 58-63% relative to GRPO and yields consistent accuracy gains of 4-6 percentage points across all model scales, while maintaining generalization on the evaluated out-of-domain task. Code and datasets are available at https://qingyonghu.github.io/AVSPO.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Adaptive Virtual Sample Policy Optimization (AVSPO), an enhancement over GRPO, to address advantage collapse in RLVR for large language models, demonstrating improved reasoning and performance.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the problem of advantage collapse in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), which limits the effectiveness of reinforcement learning in improving large language models' reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: The authors propose Adaptive Virtual Sample Policy Optimization (AVSPO), which uses virtual reward samples guided by real-time monitoring of Advantage Collapse Rate (ACR) to mitigate advantage collapse during training.
AI.result: AVSPO reduces advantage collapse by 58-63% compared to GRPO and consistently improves accuracy by 4-6 percentage points across model scales (from 0.5B to 14B parameters).
AI.conclusion: AVSPO effectively addresses advantage collapse in GRPO, improving mathematical reasoning capabilities and training robustness of large language models without compromising generalization.

title: MONET: A Massive, Open, Non-redundant and Enriched Text-to-image dataset
summary: Training large text-to-image models requires high-quality, curated datasets with diverse content and detailed captions. Yet the cost and complexity of collecting, filtering, deduplicating, and re-captioning such corpora at scale hinders open and reproducible research in the field. We introduce MONET, an open Apache 2.0 dataset of approx. 104.9M image--text pairs collected from 2.9B raw pairs across heterogeneous open sources through successive stages of safety filtering, domain-based filtering, exact and near-duplicate removal, and re-captioning with multiple vision-language models covering short to long-form descriptions, and further augmented with synthetically generated samples. Each image is shipped with pre-computed embeddings and annotations to accelerate downstream use. To validate the effectiveness of MONET, we train a 4B-parameter latent diffusion model exclusively on it and reach competitive GenEval and DPG scores, demonstrating that our dataset lowers the barrier to large-scale, reproducible text-to-image research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MONET, a curated dataset for large text-to-image model training, addressing challenges in dataset creation and accessibility.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome the cost and complexity of creating high-quality datasets to facilitate reproducible and accessible research in text-to-image modeling.
AI.method: The authors collected 2.9B image-text pairs from open sources and refined them through filtering, deduplication, re-captioning using vision-language models, and synthetic augmentation before releasing the dataset.
AI.result: The MONET dataset features approx. 104.9M pairs with pre-computed embeddings and annotations. Training a latent diffusion model on MONET yielded competitive scores on GenEval and DPG metrics.
AI.conclusion: MONET lowers the barrier to scalable, reproducible text-to-image research, providing a streamlined resource with validated effectiveness.

title: Reasoning-Trace Collapse: Evaluating the Loss of Explicit Reasoning During Fine-Tuning
summary: Explicit reasoning models are trained to produce intermediate reasoning traces before final answers, but downstream fine-tuning is often performed on ordinary instruction-response data that contains no such traces. We show that this mismatch can induce reasoning-trace collapse: a fine-tuned model continues to produce plausible final answers while losing the structurally valid explicit reasoning traces that made it a reasoning model in the first place. We introduce a structural evaluation framework that separates answer correctness from reasoning-trace validity, measuring valid, empty, missing, and truncated reasoning alongside reasoning-conditioned task performance. Using this framework, we study four open-weight reasoning models and find that standard supervised fine-tuning can rapidly suppress valid reasoning traces, and that answer-only metrics can substantially obscure this failure: in several settings, performance conditional on valid reasoning remains high while the rate of valid reasoning falls sharply. We further show that simple loss-masking strategies can substantially mitigate collapse without requiring teacher-generated reasoning traces. These results suggest that evaluations of fine-tuned reasoning models should report structural reasoning reliability metrics in addition to final-answer performance, especially when adaptation data does not contain explicit reasoning traces.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the issue of reasoning-trace collapse in fine-tuned reasoning models, introduces a structural evaluation framework, and proposes loss-masking strategies to mitigate collapse.
AI.motivation: To address the mismatch between explicit reasoning training and fine-tuning on ordinary instruction-response data, which risks degrading reasoning-trace quality.
AI.method: The authors introduced a structural evaluation framework to separately assess answer correctness and reasoning-trace validity, and tested the effects of fine-tuning and mitigation strategies like loss masking.
AI.result: Fine-tuning often suppresses valid reasoning traces, obscured by answer-only metrics, but loss-masking strategies significantly mitigate reasoning-trace collapse.
AI.conclusion: Structural reasoning reliability metrics are crucial for evaluating fine-tuned reasoning models, especially when adaptation data lacks explicit reasoning traces.

title: AMAR: Lightweight Attention-Based Multi-User Activity Recognition from Wi-Fi CSI
summary: Wi-Fi-based human activity recognition (HAR) has emerged as a promising approach for contactless sensing, leveraging channel state information (CSI) collected from wireless transceivers. While existing studies have primarily concentrated on single-user scenarios, real-world deployments often involve multi-user settings where concurrent users' movements induce overlapping CSI patterns that challenge conventional classification methods. To address this limitation, this paper introduces an attention-based multi-user activity recognition (AMAR) framework that formulates HAR as a set prediction problem. The transformer-based architecture in AMAR leverages learnable query embeddings acting as specialized activity detectors, enabling the simultaneous identification of multiple activities from composite CSI representations. Moreover, to address deployment constraints, AMAR is designed in an edge-cloud split architecture form where lightweight convolutional networks on edge devices perform initial feature extraction, followed by residual vector quantization that achieves substantial bandwidth reduction while preserving activity-discriminative information. The cloud component performs final activity prediction through attention-based set matching, enabling the system to handle varying occupancy levels. Across classroom, meeting-room, and empty-room environments, on average AMAR nearly doubles the rate of perfectly predicting all concurrent activities compared to the best baseline. Moreover, it achieves an $F_1$-score of 53.4% compared to 45.6% for the best benchmark, and reduces occupancy estimation error by 74%, while minimizing bandwidth substantially.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AMAR, a transformer-based system for recognizing multiple human activities using Wi-Fi CSI in multi-user environments with significant improvements in accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing Wi-Fi-based HAR methods struggle in multi-user scenarios due to overlapping CSI patterns, necessitating new approaches for effective activity recognition.
AI.method: The authors propose AMAR, a transformer-based framework with learnable query embeddings for multi-activity recognition. It includes an edge-cloud split architecture: edge devices perform initial feature extraction and residual vector quantization to reduce bandwidth, while cloud-based processing applies attention-based set matching for classification.
AI.result: AMAR nearly doubles the rate of correctly identifying all activities compared to baselines, achieves an $F_1$-score of 53.4% vs. 45.6%, and reduces occupancy estimation errors by 74% while minimizing bandwidth usage.
AI.conclusion: AMAR demonstrates high effectiveness in recognizing concurrent human activities in multi-user environments, addressing limitations of existing methods and enabling practical deployment with reduced bandwidth requirements.

title: DriveMA: Rethinking Language Interfaces in Driving VLAs with One-Step Meta-Actions
summary: Driving Vision-Language-Action Models (Driving VLAs) commonly introduce natural-language reasoning as an intermediate interface for end-to-end planning, but reasoning-centric interfaces face three practical bottlenecks: obtaining high-quality reasoning annotations is difficult, generating and understanding long reasoning chains is challenging for compact models, and inference latency is substantially increased. In this paper, we rethink the design of language interfaces in Driving VLAs and show that concise one-step meta-actions are a simple yet effective alternative to verbose reasoning. Meta-actions provide semantic decision grounding while remaining low-entropy, and being automatically derivable from expert trajectories, enabling scalable supervision and reliable trajectory conditioning. Building on this interface, we propose DriveMA, which combines action-centric supervised training with a turn-level credit-assignment reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes meta-action correctness, trajectory quality, and trajectory--meta-action consistency. Experiments show that DriveMA already achieves a new state of the art on the Waymo End-to-End Driving Challenge with a 2B model, reaching a Rater Feedback Score (RFS) of 8.060, while its 4B version further improves the state of the art to 8.079; DriveMA also obtains competitive performance on NAVSIM. Ablations demonstrate that one-step meta-actions offer a better practical trade-off between expressiveness, predictability, and inference efficiency than natural-language reasoning or finer-grained action sequences. Code, data, and models will be released to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a refined approach to vision-language-action models by using concise meta-actions instead of verbose reasoning for scalability and efficiency. The proposed DriveMA achieves state-of-the-art results on driving benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Traditional reasoning-centric interfaces in vision-language-action models are impractical due to difficulties in annotation quality, challenges with long reasoning chains, and high inference latency.
AI.method: The method replaces verbose reasoning chains with one-step meta-actions, which are low-entropy, semantically grounded, and derivable from expert trajectories. Furthermore, it integrates supervised learning with reinforcement learning to optimize multiple performance metrics.
AI.result: The proposed DriveMA achieves state-of-the-art performance on the Waymo End-to-End Driving Challenge (RFS: 8.060 for 2B model and 8.079 for 4B model) and performs competitively on NAVSIM. Ablation studies reveal advantages of one-step meta-actions.
AI.conclusion: Replacing verbose reasoning with concise meta-actions proves effective, offering a balance between expressiveness, predictability, and efficiency. This research advances end-to-end driving models and enables scalable future research.

title: Let EEG Models Learn EEG
summary: High-fidelity EEG generation is critical for alleviating data scarcity and addressing privacy constraints in large-scale neural modeling. Despite recent progress, most existing approaches formulate EEG generation via discrete denoising objectives, which inadequately reflect the inherently continuous temporal dynamics and spectral structure of neural activity. As a result, these methods often struggle to preserve long-range temporal dependencies and exhibit mismatches in the spectral and temporal structure of the generated signals. In this work, we argue that effective EEG generation requires models that operate directly on the continuous evolution of neural signals. We introduce Just EEG Transformer (JET), a generative framework based on conditional flow matching that models EEG as raw sequences evolving along continuous trajectories. By learning a smooth vector field that transports noise to the EEG data distribution, JET captures temporal continuity and transient dynamics without relying on discretized denoising schemes or domain-specific representations. To ensure that the learned dynamics remain consistent with key properties of EEG signals, we introduce principled constraints that preserve spectral structure, temporal stationarity, and signal-level statistics. Across three large-scale benchmarks, JET consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing TS-FID by over 40% compared to strong baselines. Extensive analyses show that JET captures key structural properties of neural dynamics, providing a scalable and principled approach to EEG generation. Project page: https://y-research-sbu.github.io/JET/ .

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel framework, "Just EEG Transformer" (JET), for generating EEG data based on continuous temporal dynamics rather than traditional discrete denoising methods. JET significantly improves the fidelity of generated EEG signals by preserving the spectral and temporal structure.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity EEG data generation is crucial to resolve data scarcity issues and address privacy concerns when modeling neural activity at scale. Existing methods fail to adequately reflect the continuous nature of EEG signals, leading to limitations such as poor temporal dependency preservation and mismatches in spectral structure.
AI.method: JET utilizes conditional flow matching to model EEG as sequences evolving along continuous trajectories. It learns a smooth vector field to transport noise into the EEG data distribution, ensuring alignment with key EEG properties like spectral structure and temporal stationarity.
AI.result: JET delivers state-of-the-art performance in EEG generation, reducing TS-FID by over 40% across three large-scale benchmarks compared to existing baselines. It effectively captures neural dynamics and preserves key structural properties.
AI.conclusion: The proposed JET framework offers a scalable and principled approach to EEG generation, outperforming prior methods by embracing the continuous nature of neural signals. This innovation promotes improved fidelity and structural accuracy in synthetic EEG data.

title: Learning First Integrals via Backward-Generated Data and Guided Reinforcement Learning
summary: The discovery of first integrals is of fundamental scientific importance for understanding conservation laws in dynamical systems. However, existing symbolic computation tools and Large Language Models (LLMs) remain limited on this task because high-quality training data are scarce and successful solutions often depend on mathematical intuition. This paper presents FISolver, an LLM-based solver developed to address this challenge. First, we introduce a "Backward Generation" algorithm that systematically builds large-scale datasets of (differential equation, first integral) pairs by deriving differential equations from sampled integrals, thereby alleviating the data scarcity bottleneck. Second, we apply supervised fine-tuning to a compact mathematical model and further improve its performance through reinforcement learning with a Levenshtein Distance-based shaped reward. In addition, we design data synthesis and blending strategies that support effective adaptation to difficult problem families from sparse examples. Experiments show that FISolver, while requiring substantially lower computational cost, significantly outperforms larger mathematical LLMs and commercial solvers such as Mathematica on challenging benchmarks, indicating a new data-driven route for automated discovery of first integrals.

AI.tldr: FISolver is an LLM-based tool designed to improve the discovery of first integrals in dynamical systems using innovative data generation and optimization methods.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of discovering first integrals in dynamical systems, which is hindered by data scarcity and reliance on mathematical intuition.
AI.method: Introduced a 'Backward Generation' algorithm for dataset creation, used supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning with shaped rewards, along with advanced data synthesis techniques.
AI.result: FISolver outperformed larger mathematical LLMs and tools like Mathematica on benchmarks while using significantly lower computational resources.
AI.conclusion: This research proposes a data-driven approach to automate first integral discovery, highlighting FISolver's potential for scientific advancements in dynamical systems analysis.

title: Reducing Object Hallucination in LVLMs via Emphasizing Image-negative Tokens
summary: Object hallucination is a significant challenge that hinders the application of large vision-language models (LVLMs) in practice. We hypothesize that one possible origin of hallucination is the model's tendency to prioritize text generation over meaningful interaction with images. To explore this, we examine the generation process and categorize text tokens into three groups: image-positive, invariant, and negative, based on their visual dependence on input image tokens. Our analysis reveals that most generated tokens are minimally influenced by the image information. This suggests that during the model's training stage, more emphasis is placed on learning how to follow textual instructions, rather than extracting information from images. Based on this finding, we propose adjusting the training weights of different tokens depending on their visual dependence to control hallucination. Additionally, we remove a portion of the training data that potentially contains more hallucinations as a data filtering strategy. Both methods achieve a reduction in hallucination without compromising response length or introducing additional computational costs during inference. We validate our methods across three LVLM variants, demonstrating the effectiveness and general applicability.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses object hallucination in large vision-language models by analyzing the text generation process and categorizing tokens based on their visual dependence. Proposed solutions include adjusting training weights and data filtering, reducing hallucination without extra inference costs.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand and reduce object hallucination in LVLMs, which limits their practical applicability. The authors aim to uncover why models prioritize text generation over extracting meaningful image-based information.
AI.method: The authors categorize text tokens into three groups based on their dependence on image tokens and propose adjustment of training weights and a data filtering strategy to counter hallucinations in the training phase.
AI.result: The proposed methods effectively reduce hallucination without compromising response length or increasing computational costs during model inference, validated across three LVLM variants.
AI.conclusion: The study identifies key reasons behind object hallucination and offers practical, generalizable methods to address it, enhancing the performance and reliability of LVLMs.

title: Q-SYNTH: Hybrid Quantum-Classical Adversarial Augmentation for Imbalanced Fraud Detection
summary: Credit card fraud detection is fundamentally challenged by extreme class imbalance, where fraudulent transactions are rare yet operationally critical. This imbalance often biases supervised learners toward the legitimate class, leading to high overall accuracy but weaker fraud-class recall and F1-score. This paper introduces Q-SYNTH, a hybrid classical--quantum generative adversarial framework in which a parameterized quantum circuit serves as the generator and a classical neural network serves as the discriminator. Q-SYNTH is designed for minority-class fraud synthesis in tabular data and is evaluated along two dimensions: statistical fidelity to real fraud samples and downstream performance for fraud detection. To this end, generated samples are assessed using distributional similarity measures based on Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistics and Wasserstein distances, real-vs-synthetic detectability measured by AUC-ROC, and downstream classification performance across both quantum and classical classifiers. Under the reported protocol, Q-SYNTH reduces marginal distribution mismatch relative to a classical GAN baseline while maintaining competitive downstream fraud-detection performance.
